cb:div.cb:mulu: Preface to the Recorded Sayings of Chan Master Yuanhu
cb:div.head: Preface to the Recorded Sayings of Chan Master Yuanhu
cb:div.p:
I have heard that the ultimate Way is without words and wondrous awakening is not transmitted; then what have the successive patriarchs transmitted in Chan?
If it is said to be without words, then what are the sayings and the seventeen hundred cases of the Three Vehicles speaking about? Yet in transmission
there is indeed that which cannot be transmitted, and in words there is also that which words do not fully express. I have often observed that our
great master Yuanhu possessed the legacy of Longshan Huangniu, and with Chan Master Shiyu was a fellow practitioner
like brothers. Master Shi received the Dharma from Yunmen, while our master was the direct heir of Nanming; they frequently exchanged letters and verses,
calling each other intimate friends. In the spring of the gengchen year, our prefecture invited the master to reside at Puming, and Master Shi came from Xuefeng
and together with the assembly earnestly requested our master to open the hall and commence the retreat, but the master, in humility, firmly declined on account of illness.
I therefore entreated him, saying, “You are the Dharma eye of a thousand sages, containing the unsurpassed subtle meaning; why begrudge your broad and long
tongue, and not offer guidance to people of the three capacities?” The master said, “There are indeed things one may cease and things one may not
cease. Fortunately my friend Tianhua is here; why should this old monk add superfluous words?” His courteous refusals were exceedingly modest;
after declining in this way three times, he had no choice but to ascend the hall. The fourfold assembly gathered like clouds, all at once listening to his Dharma teaching,
like the resounding of great bells and chimes, shattering the world’s dull dream, suddenly causing people to awaken and rise up with clarity,
and none failed to be convinced of our master’s transcendence of sense faculties and objects, his wondrous transformations without limit. Thus, our master’s every
strike and shout was nothing but a golden needle to awaken the world; his brief words and short verses were like the sound of a poison drum, heard far and near,
all who heard were struck. Those not yet struck, if they carefully read this record, each word is a true explanation, each saying a subtle truth, as if
receiving teaching face to face. Indeed, disciples recorded these notes into volumes; this too shows our master’s swift and skillful functioning of wisdom,
widely awakening the world. Though it is said the ultimate Way is without words and wondrous awakening is not transmitted, if one spiritually understands this record, then
words ultimately return to wordlessness, and that which is not transmitted is entirely received. Ah, how ultimate this is!
Respectfully composed by the disciple Zhu Qiaoxiu of Zhicheng

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Recorded Sayings of Chan Master Yong of Yuanhu, Residing at Puming Chan Monastery in Jianning Prefecture, Fujian, Volume One

body.byline: Compiled by Dharma heirs, disciples Wujin, Wuyuan, and others
Reprinted by Dharma descendant Zhenzhi of Tui’an at Changshui

cb:div.p:
Ascending the seat, the master said: "If we speak of the Buddhadharma, this old monk has no place to open his mouth. Today I happened to see the new mountain
gate, taking my staff and roaming about in play. Passing through Guozi Ridge, all the way to Huoshao Bridge;
losing its footing, it fell into a deep stream. Fortunately the old Buddha hall came to the rescue; not only
did it rescue it, it even rode back on its neck. The new mountain gate cries injustice, and inevitably asks this old monk to judge.
The new mountain gate deserves thirty blows of the staff, and the old Buddha hall also deserves thirty blows of the staff. Why is that?
Because a cart is not pushed sideways, and principle is not bent in judgment." He threw down his staff and stepped down from the seat.

The Venerable Shiyu, together with Prefect Zhu of Zizhu, invited the master to ascend the seat. The official asked, "When the lotus emerges from the case and the true command is in force,
what is a fresh phrase?" The master immediately struck him. The official said, "I knew early on that the master had
such a pivotal device." The master said, "Where did you see it?" The official shouted; the master struck again. As the official hesitated, the master said:
"Go on, go on!" Then raising the whisk, he said, "Assembly, do you understand? Mind itself is Buddha is still
confused error; neither mind nor Buddha leaves nothing to pursue. Not mind, not Buddha, not a thing, piercing
flowers, the butterflies are seen deep within. It cannot be grasped, cannot be abandoned; within the unattainable, it is thus attained; like
dragonflies lightly touching the water as they gently fly. When Puming expounds in this way, he strikes the itchy spot of Lingfeng. Recall and know
casting off home and scattering the household, striding broadly in the great expanse, lofty and towering, solitary and दूर, bare and pure, naked and free,
treading down the crowns of all sages, seizing the vital road of ordinary beings. Even so, at times he holds fast, at times
he lets go; at times letting go within holding fast, at times holding fast within letting go. Do you also know that in the sandalwood
forest all is pure sandalwood, and in the lion's den there are nothing but lions? Among you, is there not some keen
fellow who comes forth and says, "Puming needlessly exposes the hidden depths of Lingfeng"? This mountain monk would only tell him:
"It is just because we once slept in the same bed that I know well the holes beneath the quilt." He struck the staff and stepped down from the seat.

In the winter of the xinsi year, the Venerable Wanru, together with various gentry patrons, sent a monk carrying the robe and whisk of the old man of Yuanhu
and invited the master to ascend the seat. The master pointed and said, "All you worthy ones! These stagnant goods have come from Jingwei
Wuqiu Laoren, passed to the ancestor Wuhuan of Chexi, and Wuhuan passed them to Nanming
our root master, who passed them to this sick monk. This sick monk has long regarded them with a cold eye and cast them aside
upon a heap of dust; today I take them up again to renew their radiance." Then lifting the Dharma robe, he said, "When raised,
this ancient robe does not hang by a single thread." Lifting the whisk, he said, "When wielding this old whisk, jackals
are greatly frightened." Lifting the hand scroll, he said, "Only this thing is of no use, yet it provokes people of the time to add
extra talk. Last year there was a white-handed thief who, without ever seeing it, stole it away first. Now for the time being
I store it in a quiet place; if in the future there are again rebellious descendants, they will surely drill shells and strike tiles. All you
worthy ones, do not stare at it; if you stare, you will go blind."

In the spring of the renwu year, the Venerable Wanru Wei entered the mountain to offer a feast and invited the master to ascend the seat. A monk came forward, bowed, and the master
raised the whisk and said, "Do you understand?" The monk said, "Then it directly accords with the occasion." He raised it again, saying, "The fourfold assembly all know
kindness." The master immediately struck him. A monk asked, "Setting aside sitting atop a solitary peak, what of the matter of host and guest in mutual interchange?"
The master struck and said, "Understand this blow." The monk shouted; the master struck again, then said, "I often recall the days we dwelt together;
counting on my fingers, it has been five or six springs since we parted. What obstruction is there between Min land and Wu mountains? Each time it is raised,
each time it is new." Then he raised the whisk and said, "In the past the World-Honored One held up a flower and Kashyapa smiled; the World-Honored One
said, 'I have the treasury of the true Dharma eye, the wondrous mind of nirvana, which I entrust to Mahākāśyapa.' From that time on there has been
a special transmission outside the teachings, directly pointing to the human mind, seeing one’s nature and becoming Buddha. This old fellow today
at Puming lets great light shine from the tip of the whisk; say, who can attest to it? Fortunately there is
the Venerable Ruru, invited from Xinghua, who can bear witness. Assembly! If you see it, then you may
come forth and withdraw together, walk together and sit together. If you do not know, then you will laugh at Puming’s display as mere proclamation.
But tell me, where is the place of proclamation?" He threw down the whisk and stepped down from the seat.

At the Zhongyuan festival, the xiucai Sumeng and Lianyue, together with the assembly, invited the master to ascend the seat. A monk asked, "What
is the mind of the ancient Buddha?" The master immediately struck. The monk continued, "What is the mind of a student?" The master said, "If you wish to seize
a roaming dragon, you must go beyond the lizard." The monk pressed on, "Ultimately, how is it?" The master struck again, then said, "The Dharma body
flows through the five paths, thus there are distinctions of humans, heavens, and the three lower realms. There is rising and sinking, emerging and
submerging, high and low, suffering and joy. All of you, if you wish to aid those in the three lower realms, to leave suffering
and attain happiness, you must have in a single thought a mind that is pure, in a single thought a mind that is luminous, in a single thought a mind that is unhindered. A pure mind
is the true Buddha, a luminous mind is the true Dharma, an unhindered mind is the true Way. The true Buddha is without form,
the true Dharma is without characteristics, the true Way is without substance. The three are one and the one is three, yet this is still merely naming.
You must exhaust your own side and see through it; turning your body in the cold ashes, then you can fully
take it up with your whole being. At this point, bare and shining, the Buddhas of the three times do not go beyond this,
nor do the bodhisattvas of the ten directions go beyond this. The twenty-eight patriarchs of the West and the two or three of the East also do not go beyond
this, nor do the old masters of the world go beyond this. Therefore it is said, "Everywhere is entirely the treasury of light,
and every place is nothing but the field of liberation." Although it is so, tell me, what is it to uphold it further?
He then gave a shout, struck the staff, and stepped down from the seat.

Huang Chunyuan of Sumeng, recommending Ci, invited the master to ascend the seat. The master said, "The spring wind passes dimly, fine rain and birds
cry out in sorrow. Appearing and disappearing without trace, why does the bronze man weep?" Taking up the staff, he said, "Assembly, do you see?
If what you see is taken as existent, and everything is regarded as existing, you bind yourself and submit to judgment, bearing cangue and chains. Or if
at this point you cast off worldly dust and see through conventions, and take everything as empty, you again fall into a one-sided
nest of emptiness. Therefore an ancient worthy said, "Abandoning existence and clinging to emptiness is likewise a sickness, like avoiding drowning
only to leap into fire." If there is a great person who gathers his spirit, exhausts his mind, and presses on to the cliff’s edge,
letting go, personally accepting it, reviving after utter extinction, unable to deceive even oneself, then at that time the eight characters open wide,
not only knowing the resting place of Lady Xu, but also together with the Buddhas of the three times and the patriarchs
realizing and entering the samadhi of playful activity. Even so, when you come under Puming, thirty blows cannot be reduced by even one.
Not a single blow can be spared." He struck the staff and stepped down from the seat.

On the master’s birthday he ascended the seat. A monk asked, "Where heaven and earth have no place to rest, and sun and moon never cease their motion, without involving past or present,
in a single phrase, please the master, instruct us." The master said, "Today is the seventh day of the tenth month." The monk continued, "Lanterns transmit
the message, the dew pillar sighs, how marvelous!" The master said, "This is not your realm." Another monk stepped forward, and the master immediately struck, saying,
"By imperial command the flying dragon horse is summoned, and the lame turtle sticks out its head." The monk shouted; the master struck again. Then he said, "Shakyamuni
the old fellow was born on the eighth day of the fourth month; this sick monk of Puming is born today at the hour of you. Tell me,
is it the same or is it different? If you say it is the same, then there are myriad distinctions; if you say it is different, then in fact it is one family.
Worthy ones, clergy and laity, please examine this; if not, then listen to a verse: Gautama long ago already
came into the world, yet Puming has never come into the world; water at the streambed flows busily, while clouds on the ridge disperse
at ease and self-content." He then stepped down from the seat.

Informal instruction: "From the southeast the north wind blows cold and biting; behind the mountain, the water buffalo stands dull and chilled. This lazy illness has always
made me reluctant to act, suited only to holding a warm cup of tea. The warm tea cup warms the whole body, yet the breath is cold like ashes.
All you Chan worthies, since the whole body is warmed, why is it cold like ashes? If you can see this,
then you may calmly drink tea, eat your meals, and pass the time according to your nature. If not—" He took up the staff and struck it once,
saying, "The staff speaks on behalf of you all." He struck it again. A monk asked, "Setting aside the reopening of the ancient hall,
I do not ask about that; what is Puming’s single phrase?" The master immediately shouted. The monk continued, "Then it is like the rising of the dragon
dancing after the wind, breaking through the blue waves and flowing on." The master said, "Idle words."

Informal instruction on New Year’s Eve: "The weather is cold at the ninth, the year’s end must be observed; raising cups and taking fruit, all extend their hands,
drinking tea and water, all moisten their mouths; each is complete as is, all should know this. Assembly, tell me,
know what? Provisionally it is called Buddha, expediently named wisdom. To understand this wisdom, you must
go beneath the three beams, before the seven-foot seat, wearing out your trousers and skirts, forgetting food and sleep. Yet this
wisdom is only within activity and use, not apart from before your very eyes. Therefore Zhaozhou, whenever he saw a monk come for instruction,
would ask, 'Have you been here before?' The monk would say, 'I have.' Zhaozhou would say, 'Go drink tea.' Or if he said, 'I have not
been here,' he would also say, 'Go drink tea.' The head monk asked, 'Master, why whether one has been here or not,
do you tell them all to go drink tea?' Zhaozhou called the attendant, who responded. Zhaozhou said, 'Go drink tea.' That old fellow Zhaozhou
spoke lightly and swiftly, while the attendant did not discern right and wrong. Here at Puming, we do not concern ourselves with whether one drinks tea
or eats rice; we only want him to go straight in with a single blade. Tell me, is this the same as the ancients or different? If there is a keen
patch-robed monk, come forth and speak." The assembly had no reply. The master gave a shout and said, "When the sun and moon come to an end,
the year is already finished; the fishing boat returns to the quiet reed bank, gathering up line and hook in haste, waiting for spring
winds before going out again." He took up the staff and rose.

cb:div.head: Encounters and Conditions
cb:div.p:
The master cited a verse by Yongming and asked Yunfeng Jun, "A transformed person asks an illusionist; the valley echoes answer the sound of the spring.
To reach the meaning of our school, a clay ox walks upon the water. What is its intent?" Jun said, "Dreaming eyes see empty
flowers." The master said, "What about the matter of empty flowers?" Jun said, "Do not listen with the ears." The master again asked, "The ancestor Linji
has a phrase; how would you speak it?" Jun immediately shouted; the master struck. Jun shouted again,
and the master struck again. Jun bowed. The master said, "So you are a blind donkey after all." Jun flicked his sleeves and left.

When Jie’an came for instruction and stepped through the gate, the master said, "What is it?" As An was about to reply, the master gave a thunderous shout,
and An suddenly attained realization, then covered his ears and left.

One day the master called to a novice who had just entered the abbot’s quarters and said, "My mind is not at peace; have you attained peace
or not?" The novice said, "Heaven! Heaven!" The master also said, "Heaven! Heaven!" The novice slapped his knee once
and then left.

As soon as Yichu rose from bowing, the master raised his fist and said, "Speak a phrase." Yichu immediately slapped. The master also slapped and said:
"Tell me, your slap and my slap, what is the meaning?" Yichu said, "Please take care, master."
The master then stopped; Yichu gave a shout and returned to the attendants’ quarters.

A monk came for instruction. The master asked, "Where do you come from?" He said, "From Zhejiang." The master said, "What did you see on the way?" The monk hesitated,
and the master shouted; the monk also shouted. The master struck and said, "Where does that shout land?" The monk bowed
and said, "Thank you, master, for the instruction." The master struck again.

Huang Lianxian of Anzhai, upon being appointed to Guangdong, entered the mountain to take leave and asked, "A parting phrase, I beg
the master to instruct me." The master said, "Three thousand miles away, when meeting people, do not raise it wrongly."

One day when the master was unwell, Jie’an was attending him. The master called for tea and asked, "Your name is Juexian; what do you call 'before'?"
Jie’an said, "Fortunately, today I have attained ease." The master said, "What is after awakening?" Jie’an
said, "Please take care, master." The master said, "Can you distinguish before and after?" Jie’an was silent for a long while; the master then
shouted. Jie’an said, "I simply go on drinking tea." The master said, "What is the matter of drinking tea?" Jie’an said:
"Persimmons, dates, and dried tofu are all here." The master said, "Then what about you?" Jie’an said, "I have
eaten them all in one mouthful." The master said, "What is the taste like?" Jie’an said, "The sweet is naturally sweet, the salty naturally salty."
The master said, "Not yet, say more." Jie’an said, "I thank you for the tea," then bowed. The master was greatly pleased.

A monk came for instruction. The master said, "Where have you been staying?" He said, "Last autumn at Ruru Hermitage, and over the new year at Jinsu."
The master said, "What is the matter with staying east and west?" The monk said, "Yes, yes!" The master said, "No, no!"

Two monks came for instruction. The master asked, "What have you come here for?" One monk said, "We have come especially to pay respects to the master."
The master said, "But you happen to meet when this old monk is not here." The monk had no reply. The master said, "Take thirty blows and go out."
He then said, "What about the second one?" The monk said, "Please the master give instruction." Just then the master happened to cough,
and said, "You happen to meet this old monk coughing." The monk also had no reply. The master said, "Sit and drink tea."

One evening, after leaving the hall, the master said, "The summer retreat has passed; if any of you have gained strength, you may as well reveal it.
Show it." A monk said, "Just now I bowed to the master." The master said, "Not yet, say more." The monk said, "The days are long
and people grow weary." The master said, "That is not your realm." The monk said, "And what about you, master?" The master said, "The days are long
and people grow weary." The monk then bowed.
A monk asked, "Mount Sumeru contains a mustard seed, and a mustard seed contains Mount Sumeru—what is the meaning?" The master said, "Just right."

A monk asked, "I earnestly wish to understand this matter, and feel my effort is intimate and pressing—why is it that I cannot
break through?" The master said, "Do you understand last night’s case?" He said, "This cannot deceive me."
The master said, "And yet you say you cannot break through." The monk stood still. The master shouted, "Dead man! Get out!" The monk left,
and at noon returned holding the silence tablet, presenting it and saying, "I return it to the master." The master seized him and said, "Speak! Speak!" The monk
shouted. The master said, "Not yet, say more." The monk shouted again. The master said, "After the shout, what then?" He said, "Under the eaves
the rain drips, drop by drop." The master said, "The dripping rain under the eaves—what is that to you?" The monk shouted again. The master
said, "Wild shouting," and pushed him out.

A monk asked, "Within delusion there is awakening, within awakening there is delusion. What is delusion within awakening?" The master said, "Why ask such confused questions?"
what?

A monk from Yuezhu Hermitage came for instruction and asked, "What is it to hide the body where there are no traces, and where there are no traces, not to hide the body?"
The master said, "Yuezhu Hermitage does not permit you to hide your tracks." The monk said, "What is it that dharmas abide in their dharma positions,
and that worldly appearances constantly abide?" The master pointed to his seat and said, "This position is yours." The monk
had no reply; the master struck him.

Zhuoya came for instruction. The master said, "Why do you not leave?" He replied, "At just such a time, who is it that goes and who
remains?" The master was silent for a while, then said, "Do you understand?" He said, "Understanding aside, ultimately who remains?" The master said, "You
are still indulging in false thinking." He said, "What can be done about this?" The master said, "Where do you see this old
monk?" He said, "Eye to eye." The master said, "Blind." He said, "Blind, blind!" The master then shouted.

One day the master ordered Jie’an Jin and Yichu Yuan to present the final lines of their verses. The two submitted them, and the master, after reading,
then entrusted them both with a verse, saying, "This stretch along the current has neither beginning nor end; I entrust it to the lion
cub, whose roar fills the whole earth."

cb:div.head: Raising Ancient Cases
cb:div.p:
He cited: "Chan Master Ruiyan Yan daily called to himself, 'Master!' and then answered himself, and said, 'Be alert!
Be alert! In the future do not be deceived by others.'" The master said, "In broad daylight under the blue sky, dreaming, and in the dream repeatedly calling
the master—even if alert and answering, it is still a masterless wandering spirit."

He cited: "Chan Master Fazhou Ji of Tianning was asked by Layman Lu of Wutai, 'Before the diagram, was there originally the Changes?'
Ning said, 'If not, what would you draw?' Lu said, 'After the diagram, how is it?' Ning said, 'Originally there is not a single drawing.'
Lu said, 'There are clearly sixty-four hexagrams, how can you say there is none?' Ning said, 'Layman, do not cling to words
so readily.' Lu said, 'Please, master, apart from words, bring forth one line and let me see.' Ning called, 'Wutai!' The layman responded. Ning
said, 'From where does this line arise?'" The master said, "Before and after the diagram, discussing existence or nonexistence, all are
unrelated to the Great Changes. Even if there is a call and a response and the lines and images are clear, it is still not the ultimate
rule for a patch-robed monk. Before the diagram, was there originally the Changes? Why did that old fellow of Tianning not immediately call Wutai, and when
he responded, then say, 'Was there originally the Changes?' Here Layman Lu surely had another point of entry,
thus avoiding so much later entanglement."

He cited: "At an evening instruction, Chan Master Hengchuan Gong said, 'Shaoshi has no gate—how then can one pass through? In the deep of night
better to stand patiently and listen as I speak of east and west.'" The master said, "Excellent—no gate at all."

He cited: "When the Venerable Guang of Nanming was viewing snow, an attendant monk said, 'The whole mountain is covered with snow.' Nanming said, 'A follower
of sound and pursuer of form.' The monk said, 'Please, master, speak a phrase apart from sound and form.' Nanming said, 'The whole mountain is
snow.'" The master said, "This monk pressed against a naked blade and came away alive—indeed skillful. The old man Nanming
exhausted all his seasoning, yet in the end did not quite satisfy. Here, is there anyone who can speak a phrase apart from sound and form?
After a long pause he said, "Above all, do not say the whole mountain is snow."
The World-Honored One at Birth

Knowing the kindness, one repays the kindness—at the moment, how could Yunmen be let go? Later generations should not say empty harshness,
even now one blow leaves a lasting mark.
The World-Honored One Holding Up a Flower

At the very end it is lifted up and shown to gods and humans; meeting with a smile is no small affinity; the myriad phenomena are all the true
eye; the udumbara flower blooms fresh in the midst of fire.
A Non-Buddhist Asks the Buddha

The jade flute, pure and mixed, plays a new melody; the felt board lightly tapped keeps the rhythm; yet those seated do not understand
how to listen, and instead continue another tune that confuses the tones.

Baizhang, attending Mazu, returned from the mountains to the quarters and wept bitterly. A companion asked, "Are you remembering
your parents?" Baizhang said, "No." "Were you scolded by someone?" "No." "Then why are you crying?"
Baizhang said, "Go ask the master." The companion went to ask Mazu, who said, "Go ask him."
The companion returned to the quarters and saw Baizhang laughing loudly, and asked, "Why were you just now
crying, and now why are you laughing?" Baizhang said, "Just now I cried, now I laugh." The companion was bewildered.

One bout of crying, one bout of laughing—before and after both return to the ancient tune; the many are not of Ziqi’s mind,
following sound and chasing form, it is hard to know the subtle.
Nanquan addressed the assembly, saying, "Not mind, not Buddha, not a thing."
Killing without seeing blood—this is Master Wang; where one lets go of body and life is precisely the time to repay kindness.
A monk asked Zhaozhou, "Does a dog have Buddha-nature or not?" Zhaozhou said, "No."

The dog thus repays the absence of Buddha-nature; wrapped in cotton, it strikes a protruding stone and enacts the true command; atop Mount Sumeru it strikes the golden
bell, startling the monkey out of naga concentration.

Zhaozhou asked Nanquan, "Where does one who knows go?" Nanquan said, "In front of the mountain,
to the lay patron’s house to become a water buffalo." Zhaozhou said, "Thank you for the instruction." Nanquan said:
"Last night at the third watch, the moon reached the window."

Last night at the third watch the moon was deep and still; meeting face to face goes beyond the ordinary stream; green grass everywhere
is fine, before and behind the mountain one may wander at ease.

Zhaozhou asked Touzi, "When one greatly dies and then lives again, how is it?" Touzi said, "Do not travel at night;
you must arrive at dawn."

Turning the hand forms clouds, overturning it makes rain; extending the foot is originally within drawing it back; hanging up sheep and selling dog, old and fitting
whether there is profit or no profit, one does not leave the marketplace.
A monk asked Shoushan, "What is Buddha?" The mountain said, "The bride rides the donkey while the mother-in-law leads it."

The bride rides the donkey while the mother-in-law leads it; the wondrous body is upright and naturally majestic; it is not that Shoushan’s meeting
after is good karma becoming bad karma.

Linji ascended the seat; a monk came forth, Linji shouted; the monk also shouted and then bowed, and Linji immediately
struck him. Another monk came; Linji raised the whisk; the monk bowed, and Linji struck him. Another monk
came; Linji raised the whisk, the monk paid no attention, and Linji also struck him. Another monk came; Linji again raised the whisk,
and the monk said, "Thank you, Venerable, for the instruction." Linji still struck him.

Northern snow drifts and opens Yanmen; level sands in disorder roll up tumbleweed roots; for fame and rank one should not calculate the capture of living
heads, but directly cut down Loulan to repay the nation’s kindness.
When Xuan Sha saw a newcomer bow, he said, "Bow properly; because of me you are able to bow to me."

Because of me you are able to bow to me—la-li la la-li—smash open the stinking skull; the Dharma body is hidden in the northern
Dipper.

Mingzhao ascended the hall; as soon as the assembly gathered, he said, "The wind is sharp and biting; this is not a place for you to settle your
body and establish your life. For now return to the warm room to discuss it." Then he went back to the abbot’s quarters. The assembly followed and stood
there. Mingzhao said, "As soon as you reach the warm room, you are already nodding off." He drove them out with his staff.

The biting wind makes words dim and hazy; discussion in the warm room turns out of tune. If you wish to find a place to settle body and life,
you must sweep away all traces of wild foxes.

Xuan Sha said, "As for this matter, it is like a single stretch of farmland, its four boundaries clearly demarcated and
deeded and sold to all of you; only the central tree still belongs to this old monk."

Bei the ascetic, skilled in clever dealings—where will you find the deed for the sold fields and gardens? In old age he still clings to this withered tree,
unaware that his whole body is buried in wild grass.

Jianshan said, "There is no dharma apart from the present moment; the meaning is in the present moment. It is not a dharma of the present moment, beyond what the ears
and eyes can reach."

Sounds and forms stand purely arrayed before one; if you try to grasp and observe them, it is entirely irrelevant. For it is all the affair of the official household
things—do not allow ordinary people to privately probe them.

Dongshan said, "Words do not unfold matters; speech does not match the opportunity. Those who take up words are lost; those who stick to phrases are
deluded."

The fan-selling old woman covers her face with her hand; the oil-selling girl slicks her hair with water. It is not that their flesh and bones are returned to good condition,
even if they apply rouge and powder, they still are not charming.

The old Bajiao displays his family style to the fullest; in words and actions there is nothing finer. Benevolence and righteousness indeed arise from poverty,
while worldly sentiment favors the wealthy households.
A monk asked Yantou, "When the ancient sail is not yet hoisted, how is it?" Yantou said, "A small fish swallows a big fish."
A small fish swallows a big fish; Mount Sumeru rushes into the sea; when the ancient sail is not yet hoisted, an iron boat is rocked by a tailwind.
Gushan Breaks the Arrow

Those who prize righteousness often rely on reckless valor; just as they rush into battle, arrows lose their sharpness. Pitiable, the mouse-like trickster seeking scars
on his hands, still mocking the old frozen pus of village sentiment.
The Old Woman Burns the Hermitage
Peach blossoms red, holding last night’s rain; willows green, wrapped in morning mist. Fragrant spring is the best—how many eyes can truly see through it?

Because a monk asked Zhaozhou, "Having just entered the monastery, I beg for the master’s instruction." Zhaozhou said, "Have you eaten congee yet?
The monk said, "I have eaten." Zhaozhou said, "Go wash your bowl." The monk had an awakening.

The golden wind brings a bit of chill in gusts; scattered autumn light fills the roadside. Travelers in confusion still cannot return,
and when meeting others, one must not make mistaken interpretations.
Things Do Not Shift

Bamboo shoots sprout and inevitably become bamboo; fallen flowers cannot be reattached to the branch. I recall as a child riding a bamboo
horse; now once again it is the time of white hair.
Zhaozhou asked Nanquan, "What is the Way?" Nanquan said, "Ordinary mind is the Way."
When angry there is affliction; when joyful there is playful laughter. If you call it ordinary, it is like a falcon flying past Silla.
Fenyang’s Ten Wisdoms Are One Suchness

At sixteen her brows are beautifully refined; in the secluded chamber she vainly grieves, recalling private feelings; she often calls out to Xiaoyu, not without
reason, only fearing that her lover’s heart is not her heart.

Xuefeng ascended the hall and said, "Venerable ones! At Wangzhou Pavilion I have met you; at Wu
shi Ridge I have met you; before the monks’ hall I have met you."

At Elephant Bone Cliff the words are most intimate; monks’ hall, Wushi, Wangzhou Pavilion—like tiny insects crawling in red flames
of fire, like a gnat carrying Mount Kunlun on its eyelashes.

Jingqing asked Xuan Sha, "I have just entered the monastery community and beg the master to point out a path of entry."
Xuan Sha said, "Do you still hear the sound of Yanxi Stream?" Jingqing said, "I hear it." Xuan Sha said, "Enter from here."

Newly entering the monastery, asking for the path of entry—completely immersed and drowned in the sound of Yanxi Stream. Ridiculous Xie San, who has no other
method, habitually uses public goods for private favors.
Lingyun Sees Peach Blossoms

In spring fragrance, everywhere is abundant beauty; wandering guests are so many their eyes are obscured. If you do not believe the spring lord has any private
intention, only Lingyun alone was permitted to awaken upon the peach blossoms.

A monk took leave of Zhaozhou. Zhaozhou said, "Where are you going?" He said, "To various places to learn the Buddha Dharma." Zhaozhou raised
his whisk and said, "Where there is a Buddha, do not stay; where there is no Buddha, pass through quickly; three thousand
miles away, when meeting people, do not make mistaken claims." The monk said, "In that case I will not go." Zhaozhou said:
"Pick willow catkins, pick willow catkins."

When the mind’s thought and path are utterly cut off, bound up like paper and hemp—how many kinds are there? Even if one has dragon eyes and hawk eyes,
such a person, when willow catkins fall in disorder, still finds it hard to see clearly.

Minister Lu Gen asked Nanquan, "In my home there is a stone, on which one may sit or
lie. I intend to carve it into a Buddha—would that be permissible?" He said, "It is permissible." The minister said, "Would it not be impermissible?"
Nanquan said, "It is not permissible, not permissible."

Creation and transformation are endlessly extravagant in intent; where there is no covering, there is again covering. The east wind is gentle and unrestrained, with not much
power, blowing flowers into bloom and then into falling again.
A monk asked Qianfeng, "The Bhagavats of the ten directions—one road is the gate of nirvana."

With a compassionate heart he points and questions the monk—how can this compare to not knowing the arising of right and wrong? The ancient road has always been like a taut string,
yet instead it makes one walk a rugged and winding path.
Zhaozhou makes use of the twelve hours

boar, dog, rooster, monkey, sheep, horse, snake, dragon, rabbit, tiger, ox, rat—all follow the old monk, until even the plucked and hairless
birds and rabbits are exhausted; boundless elegance passes through an entire life.
Shushan asked Weishan, "Is it a phrase or no phrase?"

When vines wither and trees fall, where does the phrase return? Ancient Buddhas extend compassion without much deliberation. Fortunately, by the wind blowing the fire
into strength, suddenly within a smile one sees weapons of war.
Lu Gen asked Nanquan about the story cited regarding Dharma Master Zhao

The minister merely passes along the words of the ancients; like flowers in Wang Lao’s courtyard seen in a dream. If monks and laypeople wish to clarify the true
meaning, they should quickly take up the subtle details and examine them well.

Yunmen picked up his staff and said, "The teachings say: 'Ordinary beings take it as existence; the Two Vehicles analyze
it as nonexistence; pratyekabuddhas call it illusory existence; bodhisattvas see its very substance as emptiness.'" Then he said, "A patch-robed
monk, when seeing a staff, should just call it a staff; when walking, just walk; when sitting, just sit; in all cases do not
move it at all."

In a thousand rivers there is water and a thousand moons; for ten thousand miles of cloudless sky there is a ten thousand mile sky. In past and present mountains and rivers, people themselves
differ; the patch-robed monk’s staff again proclaims this.
The Old Woman of Mount Tai’s Saying

Busy old woman of Mount Tai pointing out the road—more pitiful still is the scrutinized ascetic Nian. Even if in a dark room one betrays one’s conscience,
what can one do when the gods and spirits are as swift as lightning?

Yunmen said, "Hearing sound, one awakens to the Way; seeing form, one clarifies the mind." Then he said, "Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva
took money to buy a foreign cake, then put it down and said, 'So it turns out to be just a steamed bun.'"

Deaf and blind, without resource, he raises this story; yet he takes a foreign cake and calls it a steamed bun. Right and wrong do not reach the high cliff
Below, it again follows the spring wind, stirring the treetops.
Verses on Herding the Ox by Chan Master Puming
Not Yet Tamed

At the low edge of dark clouds a single roar resounds; how could one believe the road beneath the feet is far? At that time the boy takes up the rope,
never paying attention to or cherishing the good seedlings.
First Taming

Today the nostrils are first pierced; such wild nature requires only the whip. One must make it clearly understand ordinary
affairs, regardless of the whole body being dragged by black karma.
Under Control

Now the paths are no longer run in vain; the whip and rope slowly consent to follow. At home the mountains, water, and grass are supplied as needed,
and one walks at ease, unwearied in graceful ease.
Turning the Head Back

By the green willow dike and the blue stream’s source, the scene is clear and the wind gentle. The water buffalo turns its head and at last
consents of itself; under the bright moon the return path is slowly lingered upon.
Fully Tamed

Wherever it comes from, it passes over here; turning left and right, all is peaceful. The yellow oriole calls until the mountain flowers
seem to smile—within sound and form, how could one not be pulled along?
Without Obstruction

Falling away into empty space, the myriad forms are such; man and ox roam freely without restraint. The whip and rope are cast aside without a trace,
leaving only the lingering inspiration of a single tune of plum blossoms.
Natural Flow

I rest in a single tranquil awakening; I let it wander the grassy path and play among fine moss. Fallen flowers reach the knees in the spring mountains,
and in the late evening still splash like richly brocaded red clouds.
Mutual Forgetting

White clouds drift across the bright moon; moon and cloud become one unified color. In the realm of wisdom at this time, mostly there is no
understanding—what talk is there of north, south, east, or west?
Solitary Illumination

With the house broken and desolate, only four bare walls remain; now clouds are still and the moon is bright and calm. It is surely not a matter of displaying daily progress along the path,
yet it is still good to press through the barrier with one’s whole body.
Both Extinguished

When all activity is exhausted, where can one seek traces of shadow? One should not carve emptiness within emptiness. Who would say it stands revealed, open and clear,
while bees and butterflies are deluded, lost in the clusters beneath the flowers?
Linji School

Staff blows like raindrops, shouts like thunder; sentient beings and Buddhas alike are allowed to split open their skulls. Since he extinguished the true Dharma eye,
fortunately there is nothing left to arrange.
Caodong School

When incense fades in the ancient hall and lamps are extinguished, in the bright moon beyond the curtain one’s steps still linger. On the jade steps, as night falls, together with the clouds
stillness reigns, and the golden-locked mysterious gate is not easily peered into.
Yunmen School

With an aura like a king, yet a disposition that is circuitous, he habitually sets up a single word as the pivot of a barrier; how could the myriad things and types
be counted, when even the breath in the throat is blocked and gone?
Guiyang School

Father and son, the whole household, are all dreamlike bodies; playing with marbles, their activity and rest are all ungraspable. When the workings are exhausted and emotions forgotten,
at that point, who is the one who calls donkey and deer?
Fayan School

Green mountains and clear waters form the gateway to the profound; jeweled nets interweave before the eyes. Once the patterns are laid out and gathered up,
all is exhausted—utterly bare, the four similes empty and desolate.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Great Bodhisattva Avalokiteśvara (requested by Shenyuan Hermitage)
cb:div.head: Great Bodhisattva Avalokiteśvara深遠庵請
cb:div.p:
You, senior seat! Sitting silently in a cold place, dull and dazed—what are you thinking of? I imagine that at that time,
finding yourself without any way out, you once stood by the Luoyang Bridge, selling off all your elegance, and on the Golden Sand Beach you once made
a marital promise; in Purple Bamboo you manifested form, in white robes you displayed dignity, with the thirty-two manifestations, adapting to circumstances and understanding affairs, with a thousand eyes
and a thousand hands—bringing blessings and calamities, responding to cries and saving beings from suffering, adding salt and vinegar, making strange demons and marvels. Why is this so?
Why? Who is not a true man? Countless, countless. If you pick them out, all become mere idle talk. Rather than this,
today return to the east dormitory of Shenyuan Hermitage, let go of the mind with its countless longings; as a stick of incense burns out
and a vase of flowers stands there, in broad daylight no one sees it.
Again

The winds and waves of the Southern Sea are perilous and deep; in the Three Tianzhu Temples there are few who understand. Lightly I hide myself within the painter’s
brush—sending a message to people of the time: do not search in vain. If not searched in vain, the willow branch still stands in the pure vase.
Again

Cloud mountains, Purple Bamboo, white parrot; green willow, pure vase, mind of water and moon—this is the true face of Perfect Penetration,
each time it is depicted, it appears newly.

cb:div.head: Treading-the-Turtle Image
cb:div.p:
Beneath Purple Bamboo, treading the head of the giant turtle, full of heroic spirit; holding a willow branch to sprinkle sweet dew, it is pure compassion.
It makes Good Wealth stand until his feet ache; even the parrot flying high is still a little short of it.

cb:div.head: Image of Great Bodhisattva Samantabhadra
cb:div.p:
He is the King of Great Vows; manifesting traces from the original ground, with not a single thought arising, the all-seeing eye is hard to find. One might think he rides a white
elephant, or even wish to become a wandering fugitive, but encountering the old master Puming, one is seen through with a cool gaze. Tell me, what is seen?
A bowl falling to the ground, a plate breaking into seven pieces.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Image of the Cloth Bag Monk (requested by Nesheng for praise)
cb:div.head: Image of the Cloth Bag Monk訥生求讚
cb:div.p:
Carrying the cloth bag, he is scorned by some, smiling cheerfully; he is also loved by others. Once in the street he begged for a single coin; behold,
you can never repay the debts of the human world.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Image of the Venerable Southern Bright, our root teacher
cb:div.head: Image of the Venerable Southern Bright, our root teacher
cb:div.p:
In the old ascetic at Xinfeng Temple, wild and unrestrained—who could do anything about his mad roaring? With a single cry, chess and wine both rise in value,
everything is exhausted; he suddenly tramples over the ancient Vairocana. In front of the Three-cedar Hall he probes the stream and measures the water; on the Five Elders Peak
he stirs up waves and raises tides, using hammer and tongs on the monks, examining fine and coarse capacities. In the end, what final instruction is given? The fishing hook
line sinks into the river; lazily he sings no more.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Image of Great Master Lianchi of Yunqi
cb:div.head: Image of Great Master Lianchi of Yunqi
cb:div.p:
Tearing open the Confucian robe, donning the purple kāṣāya, striking through all seven strokes, followed by students from all directions. He cleared away the thickets of the Five Clouds,
straightened the ancient base of taming tigers, replacing people’s eyes with black beans, cutting off life itself with
the hammer and tongs. Alas! How many times at the bamboo window he picked up the iron pen, while fragrant winds spread through the Lotus Flower Pond.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Image of Great Master Zhenjiwen (requested by Huiji)
cb:div.head: Image of Great Master Zhenjiwen慧基請
cb:div.p:
With a single heart of an old mother, he straightens out the hands and feet of people of his time; with a fiercely sharp tongue, he scolds until even blind groping
Chan study is overturned. Treasuring goodness and purity and compassion, nothing is not right; in greatness there is samadhi, in wisdom there is the foundation—taking errors
and fitting them onto errors. If you ask about the family style of Zhenji, do not try to measure it by shadows.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Half-portrait of Elder Hao at seventy
cb:div.head: Half-portrait of Elder Hao at seventy
cb:div.p:
Praise for this old worthy: his life has reached the age of seventy. He displays long-eared features and finely arched brows. As for his family style,
it is without intention and without action. He deeply tastes prajñā, meeting joy and anger at the proper times. At times like sudden thunder and fierce wind, he is no less
fierce than the wrath of an asura; at times like spring flowers and autumn moon, he is even more compassionate than Avalokiteśvara.
Though the world has the marvelous hands of Zhang and Wu, who could imitate his whole body? I dare ask the younger generations: where is that half
body? Ah!

cb:div.head: Self-portrait總題
cb:div.p:
So emaciated that he can barely bear his clothes, his mind is so sharp it is hard to tell truth from falsehood; sitting upright, there is no place for entering correct mindfulness,
in his presence there is no compassion, joy, or equanimity; he holds a white staff across his arms—if a Buddha comes, he strikes him; if a demon comes,
he strikes him too.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Painting and colophon of Layman Huang of Sumen Cultivating His Aspiration
cb:div.head: Painting and colophon of Layman Huang of Sumen Cultivating His Aspiration
cb:div.p:
Exquisitely patterned rocks, coiling ancient pines, vast clouded mountains—cranes and deer move with ease. Within it are father and son,
as in the moment of passing through the courtyard. Not bound to poetry and rites, they quietly dwell in stillness. With filial intent in nurturing, how could one be stuck on outward appearance?
Graceful and gentle at the knees of elders, joyfully lingering together; household servants present offerings—could it be even jujube fruit? Asked whether there is any surplus,
the reply is that there is no lack. On the clean table are flowers for appreciation; the sutra cases have not been opened, yet all affairs are already settled.
In this very grove and dwelling, how is it different from the Land of Ultimate Bliss? Over a hundred thousand miles away, it is only a single awakening.

When the layman’s late father changed his mat and passed away, his final instructions had long been given; thus he was able to remember past kindness and paint
this picture called Cultivating Aspiration, and also to take his place in giving refuge and precepts, giving him the Dharma name Awakening Vow. May he
awaken in a single realization, and may practice and vow be equal. Puming therefore sincerely records this.

cb:div.head: Instruction to Layman Zanruo Huang
cb:div.p:
The Buddha taught all dharmas in order to cross over all minds; if I have no such mind at all, what use are all the dharmas?
If even the true Dharma is not established, how could false teachings exist? When one speaks of right only in opposition to wrong, how can that right truly be obtained?
Right and wrong are merely names, with no real substance. Because sentient beings do not understand this, our school speaks of direct pointing.
Directly take it up at once; it is plainly the matter of everyday use. If one point is penetrated, all places are simultaneously resolved.
Looking back at these names and words, they too are dream speech; even dream speech is not established—what kind of realm is this?
Old bald slave Puming has spoken all this old-woman Chan; he should himself receive thirty blows—yet not even one blow can be measured against another.

cb:div.head: Showing to Zen practitioner Zhao
cb:div.p:
When literary patterns have not yet formed, how could one put brush to paper? Once the tip of the brush is set in motion, myriad traces appear. If the practitioner can see clearly here,
if he perceives it truly, it is like burning a red sun beneath the Persian sea.

cb:div.head: Zen practitioner Huideng requesting instruction
cb:div.p:
One's inherent wisdom lamp cannot be obtained even from before the kalpas; it arises only occasionally in accordance with conditions, and its light completely pervades everywhere.
It darkens even the brightness of buddhas and patriarchs, and yet it illuminates and shatters the darkness of all beings. I say to Zen practitioner Deng: do not take it as something special or fixed.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Du Huayu presents a fan and requests instruction
cb:div.head: Du Huayu presents a fan and requests instruction
cb:div.p:
When Yanguan specially sought the rhinoceros-horn fan, the clear river was stirred and the azure dragon appeared. If Mr. Du can also act with such sharpness,
acting fluently and decisively, then even iron caltrops would be tempered in a blazing furnace. At the tip of the flames, one speaks the unborn Dharma, and the buddhas of the three times stand
See right there on the ground. It is neither half nor full, neither partial nor complete, neither provisional nor real, neither sudden nor gradual. Sweep away all entangling vines
idle proclamations, and then you may sit in the hall of the Empty King with your robe on. If you mistake what appears in the streets and alleys as your own,
even then you still will not realize it.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Zen practitioner Zhiyi of Mount Jing requesting instruction
cb:div.head: Zen practitioner Zhiyi of Mount Jing requesting instruction
cb:div.p:
Zen practitioner Zhiyi has come from thousands of miles away, bringing letters from various gentry, inviting me
to return to Zhejiang to meet; monks and laypeople alike are tightly entangled, all concerned with the great matter of life and death. This is also the earnest concern of an old woman’s heart,
so extremely urgent. Yet the buddhas and bodhisattvas act without opposing the wishes of sentient beings, whereas this old and weary monk,
though sitting in laziness, also has this intention. But if one were to set out on the return road, with iron encircling mountains pressing from front and back,
and tigers, wolves, and lions on both sides, how would you advance then? If Zen practitioner Zhi can speak here,
and grasp the matter of going beyond, then take Dongshan’s three catties of hemp and weigh them up to the Thirty-three Heavens, and use Yunmen’s
dried shit-stick to pry open the ground until it splits in seven and eight directions.

cb:div.head: Presented to Zen practitioner Deyun
cb:div.p:
Deyun says: “Dwelling on the wondrous peak is just like trying to satisfy hunger with a painted cake.” Deyun does not dwell on the wondrous peak
of the peak; it is also like running toward the sun while trying to flee its shadow. Both paths cut off are still not intimate; grasping the middle is still
a trap. Whether ancient or present, neither this nor that, neither paired nor single. Already in Yuzhang
under the prefecture, speaking of karmic conditions and peaceful dwelling, yet in Xuancheng, he snaps the rope blocking the road and visits
Chan masters from all directions. On Wu waters, an iron boat floats freely; over Yue mountains, the moon is not in the sky. At Tiantong, he randomly
swings his white staff. Under the Tung moon I add one word to you: when you return to the eighteen rapids of the Western River,
drink it all in one gulp until it is completely dry. Turn around and exhale a single phrase—then you will see a withered well give rise to waves.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Instruction to the physician Zou of Shangshui
cb:div.head: Instruction to the physician Zou of Shangshui
cb:div.p:
Pitiable is the human world—there is no one who is not ill. Master Zou, carefully reflect: your subtle diagnosis indeed reaches the truth,
cyperus is warming, coptis is cooling—each you take up is not wrong, and its use is appropriate. Yet even with this ability,
you still lack this one step. How about the patient who recovers without needing a donkey to carry medicine? Do you not see Vimalakirti,
the elder Vimalakirti, who made those around him circle and strike him; only Manjushri was slightly better, yet still inevitably ended up
buried in wild grass. And do you not see Xie Sanlang, who cleverly raised the “three illnesses” to mock the various schools, only to be pierced by others
so that he could not open his mouth, his full intent turning into shame and embarrassment.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Instruction to the scholar Shi Zhaishi
cb:div.head: Instruction to the scholar Shi Zhaishi
cb:div.p:
This sick monk originally has no tongue in his mouth and has never been able to babble or speak. Now I ask Master Shi of Liaoheng:
who is it that relies on to light incense and sweep the ground? If one day you find this person,
you will then escape the cave of birth and death. At that time, seize and defeat the old yellow-faced one—better yet, become a street thief who steals in broad daylight. Thief! Thief! Thirty
blows of the staff you must also receive.

cb:div.head: Instruction to Zen practitioner Wuxiang
cb:div.p:
What is fragrance, and who is the one who awakens to it? If you clearly see through it, you still inevitably linger on the roadside crossing.
Therefore, with a single urgent thought one travels seeking teachers, going from Jiangxi to Hunan to inquire of spiritual friends; yet in the mouths of those teachers it is all watery talk
dripping and flowing—when spoken of too closely, it becomes hard to point out. Suddenly, when you break through the soles of your straw sandals, even the rubble at your feet is
all gold. Jianxi water becomes a single choking gulp; Iron Lion Peak roars like thunder. At that time, you roam freely
at ease in your nature, with nothing to rejoice or worry over, and no joy or sorrow to be worried about. Amid waters and forests, with grass remaining, in idle moments you sing
a little la-la-li.

cb:div.head: Instruction to layman Wang Ruliang
cb:div.p:
Lifting an iron ruyi as if it were thunder, how swift and direct is realization in that very moment? Here, in utter obscurity, there is nothing
known, and yet thorns still grow beneath your feet. When you investigate until you go beyond mind, intention, and consciousness, a wooden horse in the fire gives birth
to a foal. When study reaches the point where the paths of sage and ordinary are cut off, the mud ox under the whip even bleeds. Neither investigating, nor
studying—only then can a true man’s mind be vast and open. All dharmas are originally empty and at ease; how could one bind oneself without ropes
and yet bind oneself? Stop binding yourself—leave the hole like a smoking rabbit wearing two horns.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Layman Wu Junxian presents a fan and requests instruction
cb:div.head: Layman Wu Junxian presents a fan and requests instruction
cb:div.p:
Not a single thread is hung—just a plain white silk; no brushstrokes added—yet it is a complete piece of colored pattern. If you understand it this way,
it is eighteen thousand miles. If you do not understand it this way, you lose your eyes. Do you not see when Layman Pang went to see Mazu,
he swallowed the West River in one gulp, poured all his wealth into the Xiang Sea, and under a broken bamboo strainer
made a living in a different way. From then on his great name overwhelmed the world, his lofty spirit not easily expressed. If Master Wu
is willing to follow such fragrant traces, even recognizing a gourd as an imperial vessel, he must directly tread down the summit of Vairocana
before he can be called an extraordinary man.

cb:div.head: Attendant Zheng Yinguo requests instruction
cb:div.p:
Attendant Zheng Yinguo joins his palms and asks for instruction. What could this sick and decayed monk possibly say? Anything said would not be reliable.
In the past, the old master Niaoke was not free from falling into a snare—he picked up a piece of cotton fluff and blew it, and the attendant monk immediately awakened.
Now those who study the Way must not follow words and phrases. Simply look at the colors before your eyes: a pine broom sweeping the green of the mountain,
And again, hearing the sounds by the ear, the wind in the stream reveals a subtle meaning. Cutting off the six senses and consciousness, one does not abide in either emptiness or existence.
When there is sound, sound is heard of itself; when there is form, form is not avoided. Like an arrow once it leaves the bowstring, it goes straight with no turning back.
In the four daily postures, in every blink of an eye, it is good to take up and understand. Before your father and mother were born, where do you establish your body?
When you suddenly run into it, everywhere is not yourself. A iron snake drills into a diamond ring, a heron kicks
and topples Mount Sumeru into pieces.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Recorded sayings of Chan Master Yong of Yuanhu, resident of Puming Chan Monastery in Jianning Prefecture, Fujian, end of fascicle one
Recorded sayings of Chan Master Yong of Yuanhu, resident of Puming Chan Monastery in Puming Chan Monastery in Jianning Prefecture, Fujian, fascicle two

body.byline: Compiled by Dharma heirs and disciples Wujin, Wuyuan, and others
Reprinted by Dharma-grandson Zhenzhi of Tui’an at Changshui

cb:div.head: Letter to Layman Jiang Hairuo
cb:div.p:
In spring, I was honored by your gracious visit and the generous gift of fertile fields, which I feel again and again both grateful and ashamed for. Only this old
layman, whose virtue is abundant and whose body is well-nourished, grows stronger with age; he may be called one in a wealthy family who is not moved by wealth,
and who naturally preserves his spirit in the inner teachings and draws near those beyond the world. The scriptures say: “A bodhisattva, by past
reliance on the power of prajna, is not turned by sound and form.” In the six perfections and the four immeasurables,
every thought urges progress, every thought brings completion; one who never forgets even for a moment must surely not be from just one or two
lifetimes of cultivation. I now hear that a new Buddha hall is being built behind your residence, and that you are strict in pure practice, which greatly
satisfies my humble intention. The teaching also says: “When the heavenly drum suddenly sounds of itself, it tells the sons of heaven: ‘All dharmas
are suffering and empty, without any true reality; do not crave the five desires for a moment’s pleasure.’ One should strive for the fruit of the Way, and
realize the original nature.” But tell me, how is the fruit of the Way to be sought? How is the original nature to be realized? Yet since the elder layman
reveres the Three Jewels and fully performs Buddhist works, he may also once attend to the matter of the original nature. As for this original
nature, once it is understood, then in the streets and crossroads, whether tea songs or wine calls, nothing is not one’s own
original affair. At that time, even holding children and playing with grandchildren will be nothing but Buddhist activity, and even this original
If one seeks blessings through principle, then blessings are ever exalted; if one prays for longevity, then longevity is ever enduring, adorning the Dharma realm without
the accomplishment of the original matter.

cb:div.head: Reply to Layman Zhu Qiliang
cb:div.p:
On receiving and reading your letter, I saw in detail your full account and sincere disclosure; truly it is a very earnest mind for the Way
The intention is extremely earnest. It is only that one has not yet been able to transcend and thoroughly integrate it. As for your discussion of deluded conditioned emotional thoughts,
they are like tea and meals in the house; how could they be removed or cut off? Did not the Buddha say: Mind, Buddha, and
sentient beings—these three are without difference. Yet sentient beings, through beginningless habituation, cannot realize entry; it is not the Buddha's
fault. It is all because they lack resolute intent; birth and death are not taken urgently, and practice is not vigorous, thus they willingly
drift in saṃsāra. For those on your side, this great matter must be believed to the utmost. Having believed it to the utmost,
how could one still willingly drift? You also say: If thoughts of body and family are light, the matter of birth and death is great. If so,
since you know the matter of birth and death is great, there is nowhere that is not a great matter; why then must one concentrate the spirit in silent sitting,
only then consider it attainment? Moreover, do you not know that in the past there were Wang Pang and Wujin Danian
and many such figures recorded in the Transmission of the Lamp—those fellows without any fixed footing—yet do you not know that today there are also lay practitioners
as well? In this matter there is no distinction between this and that. If one is truly a man of spirit, raising the eyebrows, sharing life with life and death with
death, going straight forward without hesitation, only then is there a slight correspondence. As for Buddha-dharma and worldly dharma, originally there is no
difference between the two; how could they obstruct each other? You also say, If there are no affairs for several months one investigates; if there are affairs one abandons it. Since
the great matter is in oneself, how could it be taken away? You also wish this poor monk to point out a skillful means. Indeed there is
This expedient is neither mind, nor Buddha, nor things—ultimately what is it? At this
A single bite breaks it; at that moment there is indescribable joy! Even so, it is precisely fitting to receive the blow. Why
Why? Under the gate of Puming, one does not travel in vain.

cb:div.cb:mulu: To Huang Lianxian, Censor of Anzhai
cb:div.head: To Huang Lianxian, Censor of Anzhai
cb:div.p:
Since meeting at the start of summer, it is now deep autumn without noticing. How swiftly time flows! I privately rejoice that the old
layman, in times of heat and vexation, does not suffer with the world, but enters the mountains and forests alone to savor the secluded state,
but I do not know whether the original investigation has gained a flash of insight. Yesterday I heard of your journey to Shuangjing and very much wished to meet,
but I am again ill and have not had time to meet immediately.

cb:div.head: To Venerable Miyun Wu
cb:div.p:
Since parting from your seat, nearly ten years have counted on my fingers. Now that Venerable has reached his seventieth year, alas my humble body has often
been in illness; how could I endure travel? Yet morning and evening my thoughts turn to you, recalling Venerable's vast and expansive Dharma path, opening
and instructing humans and devas. If there is even one person who does not accept transformation, ultimately how should he be taken in? Respectfully
I send this as a proxy greeting, offering a modest birthday gift to convey my intention.

cb:div.head: To Xu Chunyuan (Zhenhou)
cb:div.p:
At the meal at the monastery, I received the layman’s mention of the causal conditions of Duanfeng and Dule; it depends solely on one's own discernment, earnestly
do not seek approval from others’ mouths. This sick monk, in response to the layman's question, spoke deliriously for a moment,
If speaking from the gate of the Zen lineage, even thirty blows cannot be compared as one is better than another. Yet in this gate,
there have never been words, nor any dharma to give to people. The ancients said: "If there were an actual dharma to give people, even water
could not dissolve it." How could this be deceiving people? Earnestly, earnestly!

cb:div.head: To Layman Zhu Quichen
cb:div.p:
Since parting, my words have become stiff and cannot flow from the mouth; yet we come and go on both sides, not for a short time, and I also deeply
I know you have long been attentive to this path, so why are you not free and unburdened, as a truly outstanding man should be? Recently
has there been any progress worth looking at with new eyes? Just as Layman Pang asked Master Ma: "What kind of person is one who does not accompany the ten thousand dharmas
as companions?" The answer was: "Wait until you can gulp down the waters of West River in one mouthful, then I will tell
you." Look! Where is this fellow's great mouth? Why not gain a sudden insight here
and become a man of no affairs? Would that not be the greatest joy of your life? Also, as Zhaozhou said: "All of you
are used by the twelve hours, while this old monk uses the twelve hours." Now see on what principle
he speaks such words. It is simply: when hungry, eat; when cold, add clothes; when guests come, respond
in conversation; when guests leave, close the door and sleep soundly. This is not a second person apart from the defilements, nor is it falling into emptiness
and departing. If you can glimpse a bit of this, you can also make use of it according to conditions. But if you take it further upward and grasp it, it is precisely fitting
to receive the blow.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Reply to Huang Xiaolian of Sumen (Dharma name Wudi)
cb:div.head: Reply to Huang Xiaolian of Sumen法名悟第
cb:div.p:
I have received your letter and the songs written on the fan; I read them without putting them down. Wherever there is empty praise, I cannot help but feel
ashamed. You ask about the three phrases of mind-seal, but I do not know what you call a seal of mind.
What do you call mind itself? You must not hide your roots in stone or jade. Also, in your song, the phrases about body
and mind both being liberated, and surpassing the enlightenment of a single night—on your side, can you truly be liberated
or not? Can you truly attain the awakening of one night or not? Let liberation aside—what is it that is awakened
in the end? If there is indeed some realization, quickly send word and I will rejoice for you.
How about it?

cb:div.head: Reply to Layman Huang Qingbo
cb:div.p:
I have read your letter and learned that your illness has flared up again. I have long reflected on your devoted attention to this matter over many years,
and now, can you tell where your original life-moment truly rests? I see your faculties are extraordinary; however
consider how Nanyue Rang once studied under the Sixth Patriarch. The Patriarch asked: "What thing is this that has thus come?" Rang said: "To say it is a thing
already misses the mark." The Patriarch said: "Is cultivation and realization still necessary?" Rang said: "Cultivation and realization are not nonexistent, but defilement
"If defilement is not present, it is also not attainable." I hope you can break through this point; then you may freely eat when hungry and drink when thirsty, in cold or heat,
whether in warmth or coolness. Seeing forms and hearing sounds, respond in the moment; throughout the twenty-four hours and in all four postures, nothing is not manifested
as complete. Whatever is taken up is nothing other than it; do not give rise to doubt in its use. Earnest admonition!

cb:div.cb:mulu: Reply to Xu Xiaolian of Luyi
cb:div.head: Reply to Xu Xiaolian of Luyi
cb:div.p:
Living in remote mountains, I am honored to receive your esteemed letter. I respectfully read it, and that you wish me to give instruction is beyond
shame. Yet I dare not bury your sincere intent, and cannot help but respond based on your letter,
pointing out a few things. You say the dusty world is vexing and worthy of rejection. In my view, this is precisely the most
crucial initial practice. In the midst of vexation, if you set one eye upon it—who is it that is in the dust
and toil? Who is it that rejects it? If you suddenly see through it, in daily life you naturally gain mastery without
thinking of it as defilement, and without thinking of it as something to reject.

cb:div.head: Reply to Layman Du Huayu
cb:div.p:
I carefully read your verses and see that this monk has broad learning and good understanding. If he can make his speech
accord with conduct, with practice and understanding unified, and undergo much forging and tempering with steady practice, then becoming Buddha or ancestor is not
something from outside; it is originally one's own affair and therefore not difficult. In time, nurturing the sacred embryo and reaching
profound virtue, there will naturally be the impartial judgment of dragons and devas, leading to the jeweled lotus throne, preaching the Dharma and benefiting beings, even
criticizing Buddhas and scolding patriarchs, all depending on the time and conditions. Yet nowadays those who study the Way, having learned a little
imitation language and patched together a few crude lines of writing, immediately claim enlightenment. When
everywhere deceiving others, they do not fear karmic retribution; they only want verbal excitement. Do not think that having learning
and intellectual understanding will suffice for one's life. I only fear that one day, when brought before Yama, judgment will follow one's karma and
offenses, where then is the pardon document? At that time, whom could one possibly rely on for rescue? Thus the ancient worthies
said: "In our school, it is hard to attain its subtlety; one must apply the mind with utmost care." They also said: "An eighty-year-old elder entering
the examination hall—this is truly not child's play." I understand you wish to test the truth or falsity of my worn-out verses,
and I have unintentionally produced such tangled explanations. I hope you will apply yourself and penetrate your original nature
When matters come before your eyes at that time, there is no need to ask others; you will know true and false for yourself.

cb:div.head: Reply to Layman Wu Junxian
cb:div.p:
Your letter asks how one could leave this shell-and-leaking vessel in order to cultivate the supreme vehicle Dharma. Why such words
of misunderstanding? But right now, who is it that carries this shell-and-leaking vessel? And who is it that wants to leave it?
Who is it? And what is this shell-and-leaking vessel? If you see through this, you will smile in ease. Even so,
it is not yet permitted that you have seen through it. Why? If you try to force such insight, it only adds another layer of affliction.
Respectfully admonished.

cb:div.head: To Chan Master Liangxu
cb:div.p:
Though we share the same wind and moon, our streams and mountains differ. Separated in two places, it is not without stirring of thought. I often recall the senior monk,
whose innate capacity is excellent, and who in this gate has applied effort and moved forward; compared with monks of this degenerate age, he is truly like clouds
and mud. Yet one should not laugh at those who run fifty steps after running a hundred; one should press forward directly with courage
and vigor, becoming a banner of diligence and a model for later generations—this is what this worn-out monk hopes for.

cb:div.head: To Hualin
cb:div.p:
Since parting, nearly a week has passed in haste. I trust you have completed your former vow of copying with blood, and should now open a simple
vegetarian practice and nurture your physical body to good health, and take up your own great task—throughout the twenty-four hours
carefully investigate it: where is it that is not called Hualin? Do not always
sit on the throne of the Dharma King acting out false practices and posing, performing great repentance rites and reciting the Lotus Sutra, attending today
at the Li household and tomorrow going to the Zhang household, unaware that your whole body is trapped in a ten-thousand-ren deep well,
where even your two ears can hardly hold fast. Reflect, reflect. These words of this sick and decayed monk today,
are surely unpleasant and hard to hear; yet on another day before Yama, when you receive iron blows,
if you then recall my words, it will be too late for regret. Take them deeply to heart; do not treat them as wind in the treetops. Earnest admonition.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Farewell Petition for the Guanyin Hall at Jing Mountain, Second Request
cb:div.head: Farewell Petition: Second Request for the Guanyin Hall at Jing Mountain
cb:div.p:
The opening of teaching and transformation has no ultimate origin apart from causes; before the mountain of Right Awakening, I cannot help but reveal my own shortcomings and clumsy remnants,
like leftover skill spit out by the Nairanjana River. I itch to display my petty wares; occasionally I pick up a flower and hastily speak of a separate transmission of the mind seal,
then suddenly break into laughter and speak of entrusting the golden robe. Mutual exposition and transmission is a heavy burden,
handed back and forth—how could this be a minor connection? I deeply consider my own dullness; what good affinity could I have? Before Five Elders Peak I once joined
a gathering, not without strange encounters; beneath the Great Compassion Pavilion I long kept my head down, ashamed of my dim confusion. Even now I remain delayed,
like a lame turtle always ashamed; like the useless wood of former years, I have merely followed after the ancestral halls. Moreover, my past faults are like an ocean, and my sick bones
are like firewood. Reflecting deeply, I have not even an awl of achievement in the Way—how could I contribute anything to the Dharma gate? I can only hide my form in a humble village corner,
and barely prolong these remaining years. Humbly receiving Chan Master Miaoxuan and the respected elders of Heshan, who
do not abandon my vulgarity and again send their honored letters, I open and reverently read them, as if holding the bright moon in my palm, and repeatedly reviewing the text
only makes my thatched hut gain undeserved radiance. As for the praise of being a pillar and banner, it exceeds mere encouragement; sweet nectar and compassionate
rain—how mistaken such praise is. And to further bestow various gifts and provisions only makes me increasingly blush with shame. I recall
the legacy of the former master, faintly heard as if from wall and broth; yet with regard to the virtue and presence of the elder teachers, I have scarcely had the fortune to sit face to face, almost
wishing to climb to the summit to seek instruction, but my staff is frail and I am unable to travel; thus I have repeatedly been honored with invitations yet cannot immediately
come near to receive your teaching, and now respectfully offer these few lines in reply. I humbly wish your noble spirit be broad and magnanimous, forgiving this
unworthy one; may your bright wisdom be vast and clear, and may you kindly accept this small intention. Having failed to comply with your request, I earnestly ask for compassionate understanding.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Farewell Petition to the Dharma Protectors of Puming in Jiaxing
cb:div.head: Farewell Petition to the Dharma Protectors of Puming in Jiaxing
cb:div.p:
I have rashly entered the Buddhist institution and vainly received the support of donors; I am ashamed to have participated in the ancestral assembly for many years, yet I truly lack even a single
basket of merit. I certainly have no sharp eyes like an eagle or vulture; my mind is as cold as ice; I am accustomed to hiding among deer and boars, and
due to illness only a frame of firewood-like bones remains. I guard a withered meditation practice, bringing no benefit to the Dharma gate and only increasing faults and offenses. How could I repay such kindness?
Now, receiving the support of many officials and scholars, without having carefully reflected, I am suddenly entrusted with a heavy responsibility,
with empty praise of my writings, hoping that this sick monk would accept, along with offerings of gifts, only making the Paulownia Moon feel ashamed. Moreover
this ancient monastery of Puming, though its merit does not lie in grandeur, still answers the shared wishes of the city of He; how could the matter depend
on one so insignificant as I? If this matter were spread abroad, it would make people everywhere laugh. As I struggle in a quiet room, it is not without some strange
even if I seem to be scolding the mountain spirits. I dare to reveal my sincere heart and respectfully take leave of the donors. These words may be absurd, but the Three Jewels can themselves bear witness
and verify them; my intent is truly sincere, and all the sages can clearly attest to it. Since we are mutually acquainted, I humbly ask for your understanding.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Reply to Governor Zhu of Lianyue
cb:div.head: Reply to Governor Zhu of Lianyue
cb:div.p:
This poor monk’s foundation in the Way is not firm, and his learning and understanding are especially crude. I have improperly taken a place among the monastic ranks, merely bringing shame upon the Dharma teaching. Humbly considering
that the layman does not forget the legacy spoken on Vulture Peak, and as a descendant of Ziyang he gladly serves as a pillar of this precious land,
becoming a strong protector of the region and a bright banner in literary circles. As for me, what sort of person am I, that I should dare receive such remembrance and distant
precious letters illuminating even this remote mountain? I respectfully receive and read them, but the praise is far beyond my worth, and this poor monk cannot but
be overcome with shame and fear. However, when Master Taoshan travels and expounds the Dharma in Fujian, it is widely known that those beneath his teaching platform are well ripened for transformation; outwardly
he protects and skillfully applies his teaching, making the Dharma gate as secure as golden ramparts and a pillar of the nation; wherever he goes, it is entirely appropriate. In former times
I once wandered at ease with my staff and paused for rest, admiring the benevolence of Zhicheng and delighting in its mountains and waters,
and I considered requesting a thatched roof to shelter my head, merely to prolong my remaining idle and sickly days. How could I dare to defile such a famed and blessed place
