by making it my long-term dwelling like a hanging gourd? I have since heard that this temple, though not grand, is still a gathering place for the community,
and how could a decayed and useless person like me presume to occupy such a renowned monastery? If I firmly refuse, I fear it would go against your honorable command,
especially at this time when sacred images suffer hardship and temple halls are in disrepair. Since lay supporters are kindly establishing fields of merit,
how could this poor monk remain coldly indifferent? I will temporarily accept and hang my hat there for a time, awaiting later worthies to further the teaching. Humbly
I pray for your noble illumination.

cb:div.head: Reply to the Gentry
cb:div.p:
This poor monk has mistakenly donned the monastic robe; he is nothing but ashes soaked through by rain. I have wrongly entered the forest of monks, and in the end remain a decayed
useless tree, bringing shame upon the descendants of Kapilavastu and staining the ancestral halls of Jishang. Examining myself and touching my heart,
I am fearful day and night. Humbly considering that you laymen are well versed in the Dharma garden and are all trusted donors; at Ehu, the staff
flies, receiving the initial teaching of entering the Way; when Master Shouchang arrives, one gains excellent conditions for further progress. At Bolao, the golden
needle is applied; at Jiamì and Tiantong, iron rods are mercilessly used; at Taoshan’s assembly, I have personally received instruction for many years; at Jianyitang
I am daily and nightly admonished and guided. Lotus Peak is auspicious and beautiful, celestial flowers drift with fragrance; such sacred places naturally produce many outstanding people
Indeed. How could this be unrelated to the long-circulating influence of the Zen tradition, yet remain untouched by its sweeping breeze? Yet now I have been honored with your distant and excellent letter,
I respectfully read its ornate language, but such excessive praise leaves me overwhelmed with shame. In former years, this poor monk, afflicted with illness,
withdrew into seclusion with staff and alms bowl in hand; I was unable to explore the blue waters and crimson mountains, only intending to beg for a thatched roof over my head. How could I
claim to have followed the noble traces of streamside vegetable gatherers and hemp weavers, or to imitate the pure style of those who cook oranges and simmer taro? How could there be
any subtle intention of benefiting or transforming beings within this? I later heard that the Eastern Peak monastery is solemn and
a splendid site; now to entrust it to a useless person like myself—how could only the mountain spirits object? I fear even the heavenly dragons would not
protect and bless it. Therefore I cannot easily shoulder this responsibility; it is like testing boiling water. Yet I also pity the desolate monastery,
where the golden images are peeling and decaying, and already there has been the invitation of Anāthapiṇḍika and the dedication of Jeta’s grove. If this poor monk
does not manage this matter, I fear deeply betraying the great intentions of the donors. Otherwise, one may have to go along with the situation as it is,
waiting for benevolence in the present and cultivating it in the future. I have merely expressed these rough words to respectfully reply to your intention,
and cannot elaborate further.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Instruction to the Huayan Lecture Master
cb:div.head: Instruction to the Huayan Lecture Master

lg.l:
It is idle talk to say everyone has the great scripture; that great scripture was once said to be in a single mote of dust. Buried and unrecognized, who would think the lonely
one could reveal it—yet only one beyond measure can open it up. The freely roaming elephant king often looks back and pauses; the fierce and majestic lion
may roar in vain; within this there is meaning difficult to analyze—when the pillar blooms, its colors are renewed.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Flower Treasury World (during the plum rain season)
cb:div.head: Flower Treasury World時值梅雨

lg.l:
Dense rain and thick mist blur presence and absence; villages upon villages are all indistinct—how many worlds as numerous as the Ganges sands could there be,
all together forming the present Flower Treasury realm before the eyes.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Instruction to Layman Huang Qingbo in Illness
cb:div.head: Instruction to Layman Huang Qingbo in Illness

lg.l:
Sickness and suffering are things no one can avoid—who can escape their gradual wearing away? You must urgently fix your gaze on the source
of it—what then are sickness and the one who is sick?

cb:div.cb:mulu: Two Poems on a Visit from Xie Dianbang of the Imperial Court Office
cb:div.head: Two Poems on a Visit from Xie Dianbang of the Imperial Court Office

lg.l:
In front of the hermitage, the path grass is over a zhang deep; how can your lofty carriage illuminate this desolate grove? When we meet, I am unable to say even a single word,
only hearing mountain birds sing their fine notes.

This mountain wilderness was never the realm of Foyin; I do not take the four elements as a meditation seat. It is pitiful to think of Dongpo in former days,
the old scholar who lost his jade belt and treasured it away with fondness.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Reflections on the Beginning of Autumn
cb:div.head: Reflections on the Beginning of Autumn

lg.l:
There is a practitioner who grieves the early autumn; the passing years have become my enemy as well. In the mirror my reflection appears, my appearance much
changed; leaning on a bamboo staff I can barely walk. The bright moon shines on people, soothing the ears; the west wind strikes the face, covering
dim eyes. Once again heaven and earth fade away—what further means is there now to bring this to rest?

cb:div.cb:mulu: Instruction to Huang Xiaolian of Sumen
cb:div.head: Instruction to Huang Xiaolian of Sumen

lg.l:
Fame and success are easily spat aside; with a pure heart one turns to the Buddha and wanders far away. Joyfully from the first stage one opens the heart,
embracing it, causing the green mountains to seem to smile. There is a goose in the jar that must be let out properly; without harmony at one’s fingertips, it is vain
to pluck empty sounds. If you grasp the meaning before words arise, even draining the West River in one gulp would still be difficult.

Everywhere green mountains are piled with white clouds; do not hide them away in your sack or blanket. In the furnace of red flames, if you can grasp the
moon, then in the clusters of grapes your two eyes will open.

Frost intoxicates the red trees and myriad mountain flowers; with a single brushstroke it is painted into brocade-like clouds of glow. If you know the way of coming,
and the place of return, what need is there for Puming to chatter on and on?

cb:div.cb:mulu: Instruction to Xu Xiaolian of Luyi
cb:div.head: Instruction to Xu Xiaolian of Luyi

lg.l:
Vibrantly alive and brightly luminous, yet the moment you step forward you are already in a maze; if you come asking about this matter,
even Great and Small Stonehead can speak of it intimately.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Instruction to Wei Xiaolian of Kanghou
cb:div.head: Instruction to Wei Xiaolian of Kanghou

lg.l:
Cold winds toss and disorder the monastic robe; beside the brazier, formal propriety is neglected. If you can understand it, then in a snap of the fingers when we meet,
you will grasp the intent—like a mud ox giving birth beneath the sea.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Instruction to Lin Xiaolian of Yanpo
cb:div.head: Instruction to Lin Xiaolian of Yanpo

lg.l:
At Puming there is truly not a single word; if you try to come and ask, it is not intimate either. In mountain dwellings everywhere there is autumn
scenery; only the one concerned may see it truly.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Instruction to Zhang Xiaolian of Tianxian
cb:div.head: Instruction to Zhang Xiaolian of Tianxian

lg.l:
At Puming there is a path and nothing is concealed; even the finest hair of the great cosmos is taken into account. Red trees across myriad mountains are intoxicated with frost,
their colors—before the kalpas began, if you had eyes, you would do well to observe.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Instruction to Zhu Xiaolian of Lianyue
cb:div.head: Instruction to Zhu Xiaolian of Lianyue

lg.l:
It is neither mind, Buddha, nor things; with a single rub the clay figure reveals its bones. At a glance you already know you have been mistaken,
so how could you still go on squeezing juice from a foreign flatbread?

In the quiet hills, birds play in the clear clouds of dawn; at the tips of myriad grasses lies the lavish intent of the Patriarchs. Even when form and sound are clearly present,
if you try to raise a question, it is as distant as the edge of the sky.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Instruction to Xie Dianbang of the Imperial Court Office
cb:div.head: Instruction to Xie Dianbang of the Imperial Court Office

lg.l:
A single ancient road is straight as a drawn string, yet you must know that even in straightness there is bending. Through level and uneven places alike one has traveled extensively;
it is no harm to pass again through the Puming checkpoint.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Instruction to Ye Xiaolian of Zhongzi
cb:div.head: Instruction to Ye Xiaolian of Zhongzi

lg.l:
Each frost-covered tree is like ink filled with cinnabar; all around, autumn clouds cannot be painted. Travelers on the road all have dim eyes,
so I ask you, what do you seek in coming here?

cb:div.cb:mulu: On behalf of the faithful requesting a memorial for a fire site
cb:div.head: On behalf of the faithful requesting a memorial for a fire site

lg.l:
With scorched heads and blistered faces as honored guests, brows and beards fallen away—what good karmic conditions! Thus, stripped bare,
when departing, there is no harm in becoming a Buddha or being born in heaven.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Verses in Rhyme on the Old Worthy’s “Fly Passing Through a Window”
cb:div.head: Verses in Rhyme on the Old Worthy’s “Fly Passing Through a Window”

lg.l:
The foolish fly blindly keeps drilling forward, not knowing whether exit is hard or easy. If it were willing to accept someone pointing it out,
then even the vast world could not be concealed from it.

In the turmoil of the world there is no end; you must urgently refine careful contemplation within your own heart. Look deeply into the time before your parents were born—
where then is the place to establish your life and stand your ground?

cb:div.cb:mulu: Instruction to Xu Xiaolian of Zhenhou
cb:div.head: Instruction to Xu Xiaolian of Zhenhou

lg.l:
In every moment I think of you as if mad; when we meet over a cup of wine, there are countless separations. The old public affairs of seven days in those years—
now, has the bottom of the bucket fallen away or not?

Digging a well in ordinary times is not going beyond one's duty; one must guard against thirst before thirst arises. In this world, who can avoid thirst?
Yet when the disease of thirst arrives, do not let it be too late to regret seeking water.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Instruction to the monk Benkong of Jinling on investigating the emptiness of the four great elements
cb:div.head: Instruction to the monk Benkong of Jinling on investigating the emptiness of the four great elements

lg.l:
Tearing down the eastern fence to patch the western wall, a beggar exposed and pitiful, depending on others’ compassion. Take care, monk Ben of Beicheng,
why dig into the earth in search of the blue sky?

I often recall how your household is fond of chatter, barking indiscriminately without abiding in the proper measure; after I myself have examined and broken through, I returned to
Fujian—on some future day, who will be able to examine and break through you?

cb:div.cb:mulu: Instruction in response to the rhymes of Scholar Zhou
cb:div.head: Instruction in response to the rhymes of Scholar Zhou

lg.l:
Having seen through the worldly calls of oxen and horses, you move back and forth claiming the path of cranes and clouds. What is directly pointed out is originally not
a realm; face to face it is upheld like pouring from a clean vessel. The three realms are your home, not a place of exile; your own body is not
called solitary. This wandering monk has long cherished the joy of living on a boat; why not come together and learn to drift upon the lake?

If you wish to know whence life and death come and go, a fan suddenly leaps up into the sky, striking the backbone of Śakra,
and the Persian falls in pain before the gate of the sea.

What is the meaning of seven days in the community? Before conceptual thought arises, a single shout is given, startling the Dharma body up to the Northern Dipper,
where a mud ox beneath the sea bites into raw iron.

Like insects gnawing wood, forming patterns by chance, you pass through the eighty-four thousand gates of defilement; before the eye, a single mechanism chases lightning
shadow—on the tip of a needle you carve down a thousand catties of iron.

Before the mouth moves there is not a single phrase; once one word is spoken, whence does it come? Layman Jiang, if you can discern this
clearly, lotus petals will open one by one upon the stone.

When we meet, if one opens the mouth yet does not speak of metaphysics, and does not seek forms or sounds, it is still not so; only when the crown of the head opens to the true
eye will waves surge up to the summit of Mount Huanghua.

cb:div.cb:mulu: The master took leave of Scholar Yang Fuzi and other literati
cb:div.head:
The master took leave of Scholar Yang Fuzi and other literati. The scholars asked: “What is the most intimate phrase at the moment of departure?”
The master silently shifted one step; the assembly did not understand. Then he composed a verse to show them.

lg.l:
At the banks of Fusha City we spoke our farewell—do you truly understand the intimate teaching for guiding others? Across ten thousand rivers and a thousand mountains it is vividly
spoken—why should this old monk chatter endlessly?

That old case of seven days in former years—have you had a sudden insight into it today or not? At the street of Jiao Li you personally saw it,
and clearly saw that Bodhidharma was originally an old foreign barbarian.

If you can move the primordial unity into levelness, you can make every path smooth and even. Do not say that because the road is flat there is nothing
to do—you must still see the iron snake lying across your feet.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Paying respects at the stupa of Chan Master Yue of Baiyun
cb:div.head: Paying respects at the stupa of Chan Master Yue of Baiyun

lg.l:
The gate of Baiyun opens wide to the sky; disregarding the body, one is covered in dust and earth. Supporting a frail body upon bamboo
staff, accompanied by many followers, breaking through the ancient moss. Broken steles and ruined carvings—how could they be worth viewing? Even white ox and dark slave likewise
are filled with sorrow. After three bows and rising, one more glance is added—what year will there be a return to set things in order again?

cb:div.cb:mulu: In harmony with Venerable Wen’s verses on the animal release society
cb:div.head: In harmony with Venerable Wen’s verses on the animal release society

lg.l:
The gate of life opens to a city of ten thousand homes—who could not cherish life? On this day Jingci again proclaims it,
revealing what once was flowing water and a famed reputation. Constantly recalling the Buddha’s name with earnest exhortation, all night heavenly flowers drift and
flutter lightly; fish and dragons in ravines, birds beyond the clouds—each freely enjoys its own nature and ease.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Thoughts on the Pure Land, shown to a person of Wuxing
cb:div.head: Thoughts on the Pure Land, shown to a person of Wuxing

lg.l:
What concern has mind or Pure Land with east or west? Blue lotus is everywhere equally present. Layered cloud mountains and jade steps
are pure; flowers and trees are lush, rows of trees stand low. Dew is cold, the compassionate face like a dim night moon; wind turns the Buddha’s name, wild
birds call it forth. The great cosmos is gathered in the potter’s hand—when directly seen, do not speak of a hundred thousand delusions.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Reply in rhyme to Layman Zhu Yuru
cb:div.head: Reply in rhyme to Layman Zhu Yuru

lg.l:
Meeting beyond worldly things, I would speak of the profound, yet I am ashamed my sparse capacity is not yet equal to it. At Baishe I am often startled by your excellent
verses; in black robes I lose the jewel and almost fall into confusion. By a guest window I would match the room of Ziyang, yet in wild temples I am ashamed to be called the lazy
Zanchan. You have something of the leisurely spirit of Zong and Lei; we might well sit together and cultivate lotus planting.

cb:div.head: Inscription on Xingshan Monastery有引
cb:div.p:
Since the Buddha Bathing Day of the year Wuxu I entered this monastery, and through negligence until the year Wushen I was able to travel to various
places; yet the events of this place have ultimately vanished into nothingness, which is quite laughable. In the year Wuchen
in summer, weary from travel, I again rested here. Just then Brother Dacheng and Brother Tongren kindly visited, not disdaining
the desolation but instead appreciating its quiet solitude. We examined inscriptions and records, and roughly understood its outline. Occasionally gentlemen
composed poems; though I know little of tonal patterns, I also composed two pieces.

lg.l:
The old order of the Dharma is now deeply submerged; Liang and Song inscriptions—where can their traces be found? The green-vine niche is closed, the monks
keep still; the white hut door stands open, letting the grasses grow deep. Curtains rolled up, wild wind carries the shepherd’s flute; the temple chime strikes the empty green haze,
brushing through sparse forest. I alone lament that the flame of wisdom has no new blaze, and with shaven head I often scratch, sighing over past and present.

Tall trees and a clear stream encircle the deep monastery; short bridges are sought in several places. Pagodas stand side by side, startling the morning
birds; fragrant halls are unevenly set in the slanting daytime shade. Pine paths sealed in cloud must be freely roamed; willow ponds pulling the moon stir
long chants. The eminent one who wandered with staff and inscribed the scenic site still makes me ashamed of my former student’s heart.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Reply to Chan Master Dacheng’s visit
cb:div.head: Reply to Chan Master Dacheng’s visit

lg.l:
My old friend earnestly comes to break the summer heat; in welcoming him I unconsciously put my shoes on backwards. Asking you about affairs on the road,
how were they? Smiling, you point to a drop of rain before the eaves. Before words arise, the mechanism is activated and the eye alone is clear; facing the moon, chanting, the mind
is filled together. At this gathering it is not easy to meet like this—may we avoid having clouds fly up beneath our feet.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Reply to Chan Master Tongren’s visit
cb:div.head: Reply to Chan Master Tongren’s visit

lg.l:
Lingering in a desolate monastery, how could it be called a home? Indeed people may find the village road too distant. My thin body has only just begun to rest,
yet why does a lofty practitioner travel far by carriage? Discussing Chan under the moon, pines and clouds are still; composing verses in the wind, bamboo
rhythms are excellent. Remembering the old paths of the Five Peaks, beyond the forest the sound of flutes is like Hu melodies.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Reply to Chan Master Muyun’s letter
cb:div.head: Reply to Chan Master Muyun’s letter

lg.l:
Frosty sky, cold moon under vine shade; it is as if traces of poetry brush across half the forest. Why do things and sentiments know intent so well,
after so long, already filling plain paper with deep longing? Our separation is not like falling into worldly dust; in dreams I still sketch your smiling
words in search—only cold plum blossoms remain at this moment; it is fitting to pick them to send to a true friend.

In the fragrant courtyard, year after year spring is splashed with beauty; in transplanting it here, do not mind too much solitude. Still the moonlight remains, thinning the old
shadows, while fresh rain and dew nourish new branches. In the secluded grove there is already enough vitality shared; there is no need for settled dreams
When depicting such places, fortunately it is all on deeply fertile ground; enduring the cold is originally what allows flowers to take form.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Writing on the Flower Ornament Sutra for a friend
cb:div.head: Writing on the Flower Ornament Sutra for a friend

lg.l:
Dew-drop farming in mist amid mixed blossoms; a single thought arises without cause, imitating wild hemp; in the colored clouds and blue heavens the brush moves
vast and free, green bamboo and clear wind write extravagant verses. At the inkstone the ocean of fragrant waters is stirred; at the brush tip the assembly of
Suras is overturned. Meeting and asking for guidance, there are no extraordinary lines to give—please look instead at the slanting shadows of coral trees.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Instruction to Layman Zixing on visiting Tiantong
cb:div.head: Instruction to Layman Zixing on visiting Tiantong

lg.l:
In the eighth month you travel to Yue; in autumn mountains everywhere reveal the golden wind. Only observe how the river thus sends flying
waves—do not ask how the moon fills the sky. Before Mount Taibai all is fully assented; above Hejia Pond all traces are utterly
gone. Taking up once more the intimate words at parting, at dawn the boat curtain is pushed aside and the sun is bright red.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Two quatrains on climbing the Misty Rain Pavilion
cb:div.head: Two quatrains on climbing the Misty Rain Pavilion

lg.l:
Clear scenery is lovingly present in the wheat-harvest season; a few of us hire a ferry and try climbing the pavilion. On a clear day, do not say there is no mist and
rain—empty green haze and shaded depths float before the eyes.

Water pavilions and fishing boats are each serene; painting scrolls and poetic moods spread near and far. Reclining in ease, one does not notice the sky about to
dusk, while in the salt warehouses and myriad wells cooking smoke rises everywhere.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Reply in rhyme to Chan Master Fushi
cb:div.head: Reply in rhyme to Chan Master Fushi

lg.l:
The Eastern Ji monastery preserves an ancient style of the world; relying on lofty integrity, one may again follow its traces. Plum blossom fragrance is carried to South Lake,
through winding bends—why must consolation rely on formal greetings?

cb:div.cb:mulu: Reply to Chan Master Konglin’s visit, two poems
cb:div.head: Reply to Chan Master Konglin’s visit, two poems

lg.l:
I was just lamenting the vanished fish and wild geese, when unexpectedly you crossed the wild fields; meeting, we can stay together for a night or two, and that is better than many years apart.
I grieve that the Dharma has declined from ancient times, and in sorrow over the age I strike the tea mat. I admire your humble learning, and rejoice that you further seek completeness.
With eyes as strong as sinew and bone, you gaze upon the vast world; even worldly folk are made not to fall into dust and defilement.
Wherever I go I am among clouds and mountains; at all times flowers and willows are neighbors. Heaven and earth all belong to me—how could living experience be called poverty?

cb:div.cb:mulu: Venerable Miyin Zhenlin honored me with a visit
cb:div.head: Venerable Miyin Zhenlin honored me with a visit

lg.l:
For twenty years your lofty forest traces—how did you come to this distant village? Separation only adds to the years; meeting now, we learn of each other's warmth and cold.
Our friendship aspires to the ancients; in forgetting age you graciously lower yourself in respect. I am ashamed, being but useless timber, yet we still discuss name and principle.

cb:div.cb:mulu: In harmony with the Ten Practices of Longmen
cb:div.head: In harmony with the Ten Practices of Longmen

lg.l:
Breaking free from the original mother's single body of bones, supporting heaven and propping up earth with nothing obstructed; cutting off Buddha and Mara in seated stillness with nothing remaining,
where could heavenly maidens even find flowers to scatter?

As soon as one enters the gate, a shout is given; even if a lion loses all its majesty. If Chan practitioners again move their lips,
they should receive another blow to the back of the head.

When the signal is struck in Uttarakuru, monks in Jambudvipa become busy; this sick monk has no strength to bear the burden,
so he can only carry Mount Sumeru as a desk peak.

Calling out, “Bodhisattva, come eat!”—even the Golden Ox would open its mouth without ceremony. But how does this compare with those in Tongyue Hermitage,
who with iron necks and bronze heads cannot be called back?

Mud-smeared, water-dragging birth-given trousers, cleansing filth still relies on the Anavatapta Lake, the lofty Jambudana sandalwood tree
On top, in later days scorched by sun and blown by wind.

The front step has not yet moved, already rushing after the next; who would know each step never actually meets the other, circling the great thousand-world realm once
Yet under the soles, when has there ever been the slightest movement?

Last night the empty sky fell down, scattering dust all over the ground, in the old monk’s eyes it is hard to tolerate
Grains of dust, just swept away with an iron broom

Casually walking once to the meditation couch, the rolling recitation resounds so high, those who know each other rejoice as fellow-robed companions
As guests, even a foolish old woman is not granted three inches of leniency

The present Buddha does not bow to past Buddhas; it has already become foul and stinking throughout all directions, the old monk bows diligently every day
Tell me, how can it be compared?

Mud people and wooden puppets chance to meet, furrowed brows and tangled faces, talk grows lively, bamboo stools and incense altar accompany the night sitting
Sitting through the night, vase flowers fill the seat, laughing in the spring breeze

cb:div.cb:mulu: Ten poems composed in response to Master Xue’s mountain dwelling
cb:div.head: Ten poems composed in response to Master Xue’s mountain dwelling

lg.l:
Green mountains only allow companionship with white clouds; a gourd hut lets me be buried there as well; the cold mountain face looks upon me
Vast mist and cloud shadows reveal the monk’s straw sandals

Lethargic and detached from the world, unlike other companions, I bury my form deep within myself; reckoning with past idle bones
I regret having worn out several pairs of shoes

Completely forgetting old companions, a solitary ascetic buried alone in desolation; sitting through the empty mountain with no affairs
Ordinarily not even costing a pair of worn-out shoes

At times I suddenly recall old companions, burying fame and profit in deep pits with all effort; how unlike the mountain dweller without affairs
Who idly accompanies a bamboo staff and straw sandals

Clear wind and white moon belong to our kind; mountain colors and stream sounds are buried within; in countless lives of wandering places
Green moss cushions the feet, no shoes are worn

Monkeys and wild deer are my companions; daily I tend a thatched fire; salt and sauce have never been lacking through the years
Noble households never reach the old monk’s shoes

Spring mountains after rain delight our kind; green trees and red flowers are buried in clouds; in high spirits I cross streams to climb and gaze afar
From what place could rain have wet my shoes?

Alas, within the monastery there is such a one, buried headless in a thicket of vines; if someday there is a return to the mountains
I would still regret having often worn out my shoes

Seeking Buddha or immortality, each has their own kind; all become trapped in their own ruts; in former days they spoke of loving the black pearl
Today it is seen as merely broken straw sandals

Body and shadow circling as if companions, green mountains laugh at me buried in stillness; today I sit steadily watching ease
How many times have I worn through iron-soled shoes

A few clouds added to spur poetry, a single plough of new rain brings joy to ten thousand homes, heard as line after line of peace and prosperity
Meandering bends; looking out over rippling field and wild water patterns. Under heaven, who does not repay the highest virtue; by the north window, who is not joyful
Southern breeze; even begging with a gourd ladle can sustain the remainder of life; in my downcast poetic mood, inspiration is abundant

cb:div.cb:mulu: Composed on the theme of cocks crowing in unison west of the stream
cb:div.head: Composed on the theme of cocks crowing in unison west of the stream

lg.l:
In a single room I seek seclusion, delighted to dwell beside the stream; is Hangu Pass perhaps west of the stream? The river city lies distant and dim, war drums sinking into silence
Drums; the villages bustle loudly as dawn cocks herald the day. The slanting moon is dim and hazy, its sound should be slow; the fresh morning breeze revives all things, singing
coming together in unison; at times they seem vivid like butterflies, deceiving one into hearing them cry beneath my window

cb:div.cb:mulu: Two poems on the boat hermitage
cb:div.head: Two poems on the boat hermitage

lg.l:
A thatched roof like a skiff moored on a misty lake; pure dreams are nothing but journeys upon water; the cooking stove often borrows fishermen’s lights
to seek; mountain boys are often called as boatmen. In the shade of paulownia, a sharp wind startles the awning to turn; moonlight on the window shifts, as if
waves rise and support it; before the hermitage in sparse shadows stand a thousand bamboo stalks, well suited for fishing river perch on level ground

Lingering in clouded ravines I settle my traces; the wild scenes of the vast river can scarcely be described; moonlight soaks the steps and spreads flat across them
water; high dwelling pine shade densely covers the awning. From afar the flying waterfall seems like floral waves in motion; nearby neighbors appear to host guests
when boats meet; let others point to those dwelling in the mountains; I alone make my pact of the heart with seagulls

Idle clouds especially favor the wild monk’s dwelling, repeatedly flying to the green cliffs, erasing a line of wild geese in formation
geese; confused about their return, how many crows search for nests. Lightly tinting the plum blossoms with faint cold flowers, heavily hanging in front of the woods, slender
slanting bamboo; it is idle talk that one has long dwelt and rested in the mountains, for the spring breeze still allows emergence into rosy mists

The long sky’s clouds are cold and dull as if stubborn; dimly they hang low as if they could be pulled down; without rain they should not yet have formed into frozen color
color; if there is wind, why not ride it back to the mountains? Wandering traces may briefly become empty husks, but fixed traces are not the same as obstruction
at the pass of travelers; in myriad wells of the riverside village, night fires cook and rise, chaotic smoke striking and breaking into mottled patterns

Rain sounds in disorder wash the green mountains; the traveler sits in desolation as drip after drip falls in idle stillness; clear rhythms lightly strike the bones of poetry
Cold; damp marks repeatedly spatter the patched monk’s robe in mottled stains. Wind combs through empty bamboo greedily wrapped in mist; jade-dewed cold plum blossoms, lazy
Smiling face; seated embracing a red brazier, what intention is there? In several places rushing streams murmur and trickle

Smiling as I play with the icy disc, meaning seems delayed; walking on and on, how could we speak of parting? I pity you, becoming solitary in empty stillness
departing, it draws me into clear chanting, thinking of you. As it gently reaches the plum blossom marker I try to hold it back, as compared to the sudden shifting of flower shadows
urging movement; if the empty forest allows a night’s appointment, then better to send this poem widely tonight itself

The wild monk endures the cold while watching snow fly; which peak is not covered and concealed? Beneath the bamboo, the old plum secretly breaks through
jade; before the eaves tame birds urgently seek the beam. Graceful moonlight is wholly without shadow; each falling petal’s beauty ultimately
is without fragrance; facing you I chant in lofty spirit many times, fearing even my disheveled hair will turn gray

cb:div.cb:mulu: Imitating the Ancient Style of Master Guanxiu, Five Poems
cb:div.head: Imitating the Ancient Style of Master Guanxiu, Five Poems

lg.l:
Howling winds sweep the autumn sky, roaring and startling the forest thickets; thus within the universe, things are often like being tightly bound in a pouch
Stirring through the ten thousand transformations, all creeping things are ensnared; yet my aged heart does not accept their reckless sway
What are heaven and earth after all? The myriad sounds are but dregs; peacefully beneath the clouded pavilion, I merge indifferently with vast emptiness
From this I understand nothing at all; even having eyes, one is not truly seeing; titles are in utter confusion, and I yield a great laugh

cb:div.p: Second

lg.l:
All things undergo transformation together; if not true, can it be called false? In the bustling human world, it is hard to act as the creator
Suffering and joy have their origin; glory and decay each have their dwelling place. Why are people themselves so deluded, rushing confusedly up and down
Most grieve over losing a horse, not knowing it may return; if one understands this principle in life, then a hundred years may be lived in ease and freedom
My Way is originally thus, not subject to their shaping and refining. You ask what it does? I do not know.

cb:div.p: Section Three

lg.l:
On South Mountain there is a man of stature, truly heroic in spirit; he has climbed Mount Tai and Mount Hua, and has explored the waves of the boundless sea.
His attainments are not shallow, and his vision is also very high; seated grandly on his couch, he looks down upon the toil of living beings.
Bright and full like the moon in the laurel sky; facing it I am filled with joy. Heavenly fragrance comes from beyond the clouds, and I would draw it in and hold it fast.

cb:div.p: Section Four

lg.l:
The heart of heaven and earth is vast and empty, visible yet not to be imitated; it is lodged in spring flower branches and still entrusted to the autumn wind.
Glory and withering clearly have no two meanings; thus the ancient noble ones saw all things as themselves.
Living beings have never been different; it is only that they do not know restraint. Who would carefully ponder this and return to the principle of ceaseless generation?

cb:div.p: Section Five

lg.l:
Modern friendships and ancient friendships share the same principle; why distinguish old and new? Only observe what one does.
The Way and emotion interweave; each direction has its kind. If one takes the Way as intent, where would there be anything other than right relation?
Because those who hold to the Way are few, even face to face they are already apart; most are attached to emotion, outwardly sincere yet inwardly false.
Thus there is no intent to share gold; in a single morning it is lightly abandoned. Alas, I hesitate again, as long as the clear wind has not yet fallen.
If one can have the conduct of pine and cypress, I wish to form companionship with such a one.

The family tradition of frugality is not my stinginess; from of old, expenses come from the human world. One must be ashamed that the practice of the Way is largely neglected
and slackened; do not frequently visit wealthy patrons. Simply remedy hunger and injury with coarse food, and carefully preserve this illusory body
in remote mountains. If you wish to truly know it step by step at the very root, how could you lightly let your effort be set aside in idleness?

When opening a text, do not let it slip by as swiftly as shooting stars; when speaking aloud and pronouncing words, they must be clear and harmonious. Before the scriptures, if the mind is blocked and cramped like empty space
phrases, let me ask you: do you understand it now?

With a snap of the fingers, enter the latrine with calm dignity; arranging matters so even dripping water is properly placed; from the rear rack of the bucket hoop comes a single sound
of a sudden burst; one picks up the two brows of ancient worthies

To travel and inquire one must have the eye of inquiry; in chaotic shouts and wild gestures one must discern what is true; having worn through straw sandals one should oneself
cherish them; do not be greedy in wandering tirelessly among mountains and rivers

Do not say it is easy to serve guests and offer tea; in Dou Lao’s time a single word went astray; the breakthrough did not come because of Han Da
Bo; how could one so easily distinguish dragon from snake

Leaving home is surely not by chance; diligent practice must strive to be first; one must see for oneself that a disciple of the Buddha is not easily
so; the heavy burden must be carefully shouldered

When coarse delusive ignorance is laid down, in humble study one must bow the head low; if one can indeed set a fixed term for the practice of the Way
to accomplish it, one should properly regulate conduct and seek the discipline of the precepts

With steady and reliable conduct, travel to various places; lodging at temples is no hindrance to requesting entry into the hall; straighten the backbone as if in ice and snow
cold; how could one endure idle chatter that wastes time

cb:div.cb:mulu: Five Poems on Wealth, Sex, Fame, Food, and Sleep
cb:div.head: Five Poems on Wealth, Sex, Fame, Food, and Sleep

lg.l:
Gold, silver bullion, and green cash coins, amassed like mountains, are still pitiable; it is laughable that the old Pang could pour out the sea
at its bottom; how could there be any harm in simply following whatever is needed according to circumstances

Clearly a charming and radiant branch of flower; whoever encounters it cannot help but cover their eyes; this is always the old monk’s affection
for a long time; at times I alone reach toward it in the mirror

A formless thing fills the nine realms; the wise and foolish, the honored and base, each is fully filled within the heart; who can escape
from such a rut; I too am entirely buried within it

Do not speak lightly of coarse food filling the skin-bag; do not envy fine dishes, for their taste is sweet of itself; on the tongue, coarse and fine become one
taste; delight and aversion arise of themselves, without any concealment or confusion

Before dusk one already wants to sleep; half-awake at dawn one still craves slumber; in hazy dimness all things are without cause
and clarity; waking and sleeping together are stirred into one mass

cb:div.p:
Hanging the Cloud Board. The master said: The cloud board hangs high, the signal of the monastery; all with eyes can see it, yet those who know gratitude
are few; all with ears can hear it, yet those who betray gratitude are many. Suddenly he raised the mallet and said: Great virtuous ones, is there anyone who can strike
a blow? Yet the handle is in this mountain monk’s hand. Then he struck once and said: One strike
shatters empty space into dust; all copper-headed and iron-faced ones return

cb:div.cb:mulu: Preface to the Verses of the Perfect Enlightenment Society
cb:div.head: Preface to the Verses of the Perfect Enlightenment Society
cb:div.p:
The Golden-Faced Gautama is accustomed to digging caves in empty space; the twelve great bodhisattvas all seek ice within flames
Where is there to seek the monastery of Perfect Enlightenment? What do you call the teachings of the sutras? Now you good people yourselves
can eat your fill of white rice and drain the South Lake; why not take it upon your whole body, yet still wish to form a society and cultivate
practice? This is precisely what is called in broad daylight under the blue sky, being possessed by ghosts and demons. Even so, you good people still
are unwilling to hastily accept it, and do not fall into the careless confusions of the present age; this is truly rare. Slowly they take out their hands
scrolls, join palms in reverence, and ask me for written words of instruction; even this sick monk cannot avoid following delusion and chasing evil
I will briefly add a few words, and further append a verse: “In equal peace and dwelling, how should one act? In streets and alleys, do not
be careless and procrastinate; suddenly break through the road home, and be able to swallow South Lake in a single gulp.”

cb:div.head: Preface to the Flower-raising Society
cb:div.p:
From the moment the World-Honored One was born, strange and unusual, wild and unrestrained, he strode forth boldly and freely,
speaking grandly and widely, as if spitting blood upon others and so defiling his own mouth. Then at the foot of the Mountain of Right Awakening,
seeing a star he awakened to the Way, and exclaimed: “Marvelous are beings! They all possess the wisdom and virtue of the Thus Come One.” Thus he
spoke sudden and gradual teachings, spoke emptiness and existence, and it became increasingly unendurable. When it came to the very end, there was still
one remaining burden not yet discarded; amid the assembly he picked up a flower, and at that time a hundred
thousand beings of gods and humans, each and every one full of vigor—yet who looked with the true eye? Still it was slightly lacking. Occasionally
there was the golden ascetic who broke into a faint smile, as if the household could not restrain itself and had produced such a rebellious child
coming forth. He also said: “I have the treasury of the true Dharma eye, the wondrous mind of nirvana, entrusted to Mahakashyapa.” Again
and again it falls into ruin. Fortunately now the lay followers of Zuili each have eyes that can swallow the Buddhas and patriarchs, covering all regions
about to capture and defeat it; two old rogues have formed a Chan society, called Flower-raising. This is truly like
a lotus in fire, rare in the world. I dwell in a corner of South Lake village, ill and staying put, eating full meals
and dozing off to pass the days, having done nothing for a long time. Receiving the laymen’s request to consult this sick
monk, I had no choice but to scribble a rough piece. If there are any nearby who are unwilling to accept it,
break this sick monk’s legs and give him a thorough beating with a rotten club, I would have no excuse.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Preface to the Verse on Installing an Image of Great Compassion in the Xingchan Monastery Hall
cb:div.head: Preface to the Verse on Installing an Image of Great Compassion in the Xingchan Monastery Hall
cb:div.p:
I bow in homage to Great Compassion; through its pervasive fragrance one attains perfect penetration; it manifests without end, and is not bound by any place
Now at Xingchan Monastery it again appears in the world to teach the Dharma; all birds become parrots—how is this different from the Small White Flower
Carving and gold lacquer, costing as much as thousands in gold; may it spread into the minds of the multitude, who keep in contemplation the image of the Bodhisattva
In the minds of millions, there are millions of Bodhisattvas; each is not different from the other, all are the Venerable of Perfect Penetration
Naturally a joyful mind arises, aiding those who establish this cause; whether copper coins or green coins, what matter is it whether much or little
All faithful followers receiving this list should encourage one another; donations gathered like grains of sand will surely result in superior merit and virtue.
This is planting the cause of Perfect Penetration, which will surely yield the fruit of Perfect Penetration; these few verses are truly not empty or false words.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Preface to the fundraising for robes and alms bowl by attendant Jingyuan
cb:div.head: Preface to the fundraising for robes and alms bowl by attendant Jingyuan
cb:div.p:
Our Buddha, out of compassionate grace, taught the disciplinary law for a time; Upali followed his path and extended the monastic regulations into the latter age.
Now the gate of emptiness is desolate; not even a single alms bowl is complete; worldly conditions are sparse and declining—how can the three robes be provided? From great heights
drifting silk threads cannot yet be drawn through a needle to make thread; cloud fragments in midair do not allow scissors to descend and form garments.
It is necessary to rely on offerings from faithful lay supporters, so that the morning sun will not patch the ragged robe; thus even idle monks
may remain pure, and gladly face the moon while finishing the remaining scriptures. This wandering ascetic truthfully reports that
this merit is true merit.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Petition for the fundraising of bell and drum
cb:div.head: Petition for the fundraising of bell and drum
cb:div.p:
The Jingci Hermitage was founded by Great Master Wen of Taoshan in western Zhejiang. The master, carrying
both urgency in discipline and broad propagation of Chan and Pure Land, traveled everywhere with his staff, transforming and guiding both monastics and laity; especially through his actionless activity
he moved like a blade in open space. Wherever he arrived at Xintin and Jiantin, countless red banners were raised. Moreover
in the samadhi of compassionate mind, he took pity on all beings in water and on land, flying and crawling creatures, and each month initiated the release of life
protection societies, thus establishing places for releasing life. Yet while urgently saving beings as if one’s eyebrows were on fire, regarding
the instruments of the Dharma not yet complete, this is self-evident. Thus monasteries are established and bells and drums prepared. Alas!
Is it merely a matter of monasteries? It depends on having the right people. Is it merely a matter of bells and drums? It depends on spreading the Dharma.
Recalling the spiritual Vulture Peak of Kapilavastu, whenever the Great Enlightened World-Honored One opened a Dharma assembly, he would first sound the bell to gather the assembly,
and beat the drum to ascend the platform, intending that all beings on earth would realize before words are spoken and understand beyond sound in an instant,
in an instant go from delusion to awakening, transform from ordinary to holy; such instruments and their Dharma benefits are vast and extensive—how could
their scope be easily measured? Thus in the Jetavana grove, the striking of the bell once revealed the nature of hearing; at Grdhrakuta
Mountain, the great Dharma drum was sounded to proclaim immeasurable meanings; these are standards unerased through the ages. Now this hermitage
is entrusted to so-and-so as its senior steward. We all consider ourselves unworthy descendants, yet we must follow in the noble traces
Suddenly, with the intent of Perfect Penetration, they cast a drum, and with a heart of raw iron they cast a bell, not wishing to rely solely on the assent of
individual effort to accomplish the task, but instead wishing to fully bow before the gathering of myriad conditions; observing their intention, it is truly good. How could
there be any wise lay supporters who would not gladly take up the brush and joyfully contribute? Hearing this, I am greatly pleased, and say
there is now a monastery, and there are now bells and drums; therefore, without avoiding lack of literary elegance, I place this at the beginning of the scroll.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Revision of the Commentary on the Flower Ornament Sutra
cb:div.head: Revision of the Commentary on the Flower Ornament Sutra
cb:div.p:
The great master Nagarjuna took up a small portion of the Garland of Flowers, and the venerable Yutian translated it into hundreds of billions of
thousands of words. In the land of China, all is the body of Vairocana; is not the Eastern Radiance Hall itself the realm of the Flower Treasury? The mounting
having lasted long, heavenly silk brocades are nearly turning to ashes; as years repeatedly pass, flying squirrels and bookworms are about to feast their fill.
The Chan practitioner Lang deeply laments this and wishes to shoulder the task of repair; the stinginess of lay households will thereby naturally be broken,
and he requests that the storerooms be opened and the boxes unsealed. Know that this is a good karmic condition in response; surely, when the form is upright the shadow will be straight, and the planting
of blessings will return with abundant fruits of merit; responses will surely be harmonious like echo following sound. Reading these few words, who would not gladly offer support?

cb:div.cb:mulu: Postface to the Dharma Instructions of Chan Master Zhongfeng Given to Magistrate Zhuang
cb:div.head: Postface to the Dharma Instructions of Chan Master Zhongfeng Given to Magistrate Zhuang
cb:div.p:
To the northwest of Tong county, about half a stage away, there is a hermitage called Huaiyuan. Within it is a monk called Zhongyan, who
is my Dharma brother of the same lineage; he has long traveled and inquired of various masters, and has already consented to rest his wandering. One day, by chance,
he picked up a scrap of paper among crumbling dusty walls and carefully examined it; it was the handwritten Dharma words of the Old Man of Illusory Dwelling,
left for me to read. I said: “The old master’s complete writings are full of tangled vines—how could this single passage be missing?” It is especially
absent even from the extensive recorded sayings, which is even more strange, so I have treasured it for a long time. Just then, Elder Xu
and another lay practitioner came to the hermitage to undertake a seven-day meditation retreat; they joined palms and requested instruction, while my tongue was already worn out and
my throat hoarse, so I merely used this to respond. The two gentlemen, upon seeing it, were unexpectedly delighted and immediately ordered it to be preserved,
treating it as if it were jade though it is only stone. Alas! One blind person leads many blind people away.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Postface by the Filial Licentiate, Layman Huang of Sumen, to the Dharma Instructions for Gentry by Old Patriarch Foguog
cb:div.head:
Dharma Instructions for Gentry by Old Patriarch Foguog, written by the Filial Licentiate, Layman Huang of Sumen
Postface
cb:div.p:
Old Patriarch Foguog is regarded as the eleventh generation from Linji. Outstanding in the Zen lineage, his insight is transcendent, therefore
his responses to conditions and teachings are like a bright pearl rolling on a tray, vivid and free, direct and incisive, pointing straight to the essence
and guiding accordingly; thus his Dharma instructions to literati and officials fill many scrolls, how could they be limited? Now I select this
one piece, which pierces worldly affairs, intimate and simple, truly well expounding the essential Dharma. Even so, it is still
a provisional teaching for a certain time, still somewhat lingering on the lips and teeth. Now since the layman wishes to see through the dust of the world and
become a true master of the household, he must have the swift hawk eye that instantly sees through the ground, somersault in empty space, and break through the enclosures of the ancients
and painted confines; sitting and cutting off even the heads of reward and transformation Buddhas, he can then seize and block the vital passes of the eight great crossroads, using stick and shout
of past and present, scolding Buddhas and patriarchs—yet one may still say that Puming does not know good or evil, speaking of long and short, saying
this and that, binding and engraving people’s good men and good women into the karma of birth and death. At that time, Puming
can only let you step forward. Even so, there is still a blunt axe, and the handle is still
in Puming’s hand; if you can utter a truly intimate phrase, it will be handed over to you with both hands.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Inscription on the Diamond Pagoda Written in Blood
cb:div.head: Inscription on the Diamond Pagoda Written in Blood
cb:div.p:
Who is Master Xi? He has attained the power of the samadhi of illusion; he regards the body as a bubble shadow and blood as spittle.
His carving finds no joints to enter, his brush moves like a swift wind; not a single dot is added to the text, not a single stroke is misplaced.
Thus he writes this sutra, which is also called this pagoda. When it is raised up, the ringing wind resounds on its own, coral brilliance
glows in the cold of night light; the stone man sleeps with bent arm without dreams, while the bright moon often comes to gaze in silence.

cb:div.head: Record of the Crab-Stone Rack
cb:div.p:
A sage suddenly arose, manifesting wondrous auspicious signs; from what mountain’s spirit does this Crab Stone come? With a little skilled craftsmanship,
it becomes naturally exquisite and translucent, with openings on all sides and water flowing through. From ancient times to now, it has been called the Crab-Stone Rack,
before names and forms arose there was not the slightest seam; when crab is not seen as crab and stone is not seen as stone, once names and forms arise
even a tortoise is taken as a soft-shelled turtle.

cb:div.head: Inscription for the Sutra Casket
cb:div.p:
This kitchen vessel too is named according to circumstance; in principle it is originally empty, yet it can contain the sutras. Though its form is quiet and silent,
it vividly proclaims and spreads the teaching; I bow in homage to this named vessel, which is indeed the great jewel king.

cb:div.head: Inscription on Eyeglasses
cb:div.p: When this is picked up, light becomes manifest; every mote of dust across the great earth is completely laid bare and examined.

cb:div.cb:mulu: Sending off the monk Ke on his pilgrimage
cb:div.head: Sending off the monk Ke on his pilgrimage
cb:div.p:
On the distant road yet to be traveled, why worry about a single step of the heel? A broken gourd and ragged robe are utter freedom.
In the moon one’s shadow is reflected in mourning; beyond things one keeps company with water and clouds. The ladle handle, east or west, long or short,
shallow or deep at the stream’s head; what is before the eyes is gathered without intent before the eyes. Rice fragrance over a thousand acres of green; cold mist over ten thousand homes in autumn.

cb:div.cb:mulu: In response to Su Dongpo’s rhyme on selling new silk in the second month and new grain in the fifth
cb:div.head: In response to Su Dongpo’s rhyme on selling new silk in the second month and new grain in the fifth
cb:div.p:
I resent that creation is too unjust; in the human world there is only prosperity and decline. I was born among farmsteads with mulberries and cudrania trees
and plowed fields of grain; in recent years I have seldom seen full ears, and the granaries are utterly empty. Men and women and family
relations—how few there are, struggling just to muddle through. In the second month of spring one sells new silk in advance, temporarily to
patch the mouth; how can one wait for delay until the autumn rent ripens? Even rice seedlings are planted on borrowed new grain; who knows my feelings
of intense heart-suffering and hollowed anguish? I sigh that past causes have formed habits, and misery rushes on all day without pause.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: End of the Recorded Sayings, Volume Lower, of Chan Master Yong from Yuan Lake, abbot of Puming Chan Monastery in Jianning Prefecture, Fujian

cb:div.head: Biography of Chan Master Yuan Lake of Puming Monastery in Jianning
cb:div.p:
The master, personal name Miaoyong, style Yuanhu, was a son of the Zheng family of Yanguan; he was born in the Dinghai year of the Wanli reign at the tenth
month, seventh day, at the hour of You. In youth he disliked meat and fish, and aspired to transcend the mundane; at twelve he went to Xingchan
Monastery and took Jizhou as his master. At seventeen he shaved his head; constantly reflecting on how there was no way to escape birth and death, he went to pay respects to
the Old Man Wuhuan of Chexi and sought instruction on the essentials of investigation. He then went up to Jing Mountain to attend the great master Nanming
master. One day he happened to read the Sutra of Brahma Heaven Asked for Instruction and had an awakening, presenting a verse; Nanming saw it and reprimanded him, and thus
He did not compose verses. Later, upon seeing Baiyun Duan bring up the Fifth Patriarch, and clearly receiving the descending clear wind, he finally broke free
and became unobstructed. In the autumn of Gengshen, Nan was gravely ill; he raised Xiangyan’s verse to question the master. Just as he was about to speak, Nan
Shouwei gave a thunderous shout. When the master again tried to speak, Nan shouted once more; the master then nodded, and Nan immediately entrusted him with
the verse: “No transmission, no receiving Dharma; no transmission, no receiving mind. It is entrusted to the one without hands, who pulls apart the sinews of empty
space.” He further instructed: “You must maintain this; in the future, if you obtain even one or half a person, you may continue the lineage of Broken Bridge
one line, and not fail the entrustment of former worthies.” Then Nan passed away; after the master completed the funeral affairs, he only
carried his staff and hat, following conditions with a dry and simple naturalness; sometimes by the water or under trees, sometimes alone in Lu hermitage, sometimes leading
retreating into the rosy canopy, so that people could not fathom him. In the year Yihai, the official Cao of Shicang in He prefecture and others
invited the master to take the abbacy at Puming; after nearly a year he finally took up his staff and went to Fujian, concealing his traces in the mountains of Jiyang
for three years. In the spring of Gengchen, the gentry Zhu Lianyue and Huang Sumen respectfully invited him together with the assembly to reside at
Yunfeng Puming; at that time monks gathered like wheels to spokes, and the master examined and debated them, but none were in accord. In the winter of Xinsi,
Venerable Shiyu led the lay supporters in earnestly requesting him to open the Dharma, and the master, unable to refuse, ascended the seat to instruct the assembly.
He expounded the unresolved case of Master Nan; both monastics and laity in Fujian all revered him. The censorial official An
Zhai Huang took leave and asked for a final phrase before departure, requesting the master’s instruction. The master said: “Three thousand miles away,
when meeting people, do not misapply it.” In the year Renwu he showed illness, asked for a brush, and wrote a verse: “In life it is mistaken, in death it is
mistaken; the iron lion snaps the golden rope.” He threw down the brush and said “De!” and thus passed away. At that time, the fifteenth year of Chongzhen
on the tenth month, eleventh day, at the hour of Chen; his worldly age was fifty-six, and his monastic seniority was thirty-
nine years, with three Dharma heirs. Following his final instructions, his relics were interred in the original mountain, and later respectfully placed in a stupa
behind Xingchan Monastery. The master’s conduct was outstanding; his frame was lean and pure; his comings and goings were measured; in speech and laughter
he observed propriety of timing; thus many students looked up at the cliff and withdrew. Even those with a little understanding, he would not readily
approve. Now I have briefly recorded his outline, so that later readers may know how highly the master was esteemed.
Memorial presented in reverence by junior monk Wujin of Jinming

cb:div.head:
At Puming Hall on Purple Cloud Mountain in Jianning, Fujian, holder of the orthodox Linji lineage, thirtieth generation
Stupa Inscription for Chan Master Yong of Yuan Lake
cb:div.p:
The Chan master Jiean Jin of the Dharma hall of Jinming Monastery in Zuili, respectfully, on behalf of his root teacher, Chan Master Yong of Yuan Lake,
submits the Recorded Sayings and Biography of the Venerable, as well as the Biography of the Old Master Xuanwei written by Layman Liyan, and Gao
Pavilion Chan Master Xuliangming’s Record of Subtle Expositions of the Dharma Gate, requesting the Daoist of Sweeping to compose an inscription
for the stupa, in order to record its immortality. It is because in former times the Venerable Yuan and the Daoist of Sweeping, at Bai Zhu Puming
abandoned monastery, early on met through the Buddha Dharma and lingered together in humble circumstance for more than a day. To be able to
compose this final inscription—if not the Daoist of Sweeping, who else? Since Master Jie, together with Chan Master Yichuyuan, from
times of great upheaval and war, traveled thousands of miles back and forth, carrying the sacred relics back to He, and again, together with Fusheng
Xin and Layman Bai Kai, selected the site and built the stupa at the old Xingchan monastery, thus bringing the matter to final completion.
Initially, in the case of Venerable Yuan, it was a return to origin; in the case of Masters Jie and Yiliang, it was filial upholding of the root of the Way,
one may say they were good at continuing and transmitting. The Daoist of Sweeping thoroughly read the biographies and records, and saw the Way of Venerable Yuan
understanding; further reading the Record of Manifest Subtleties, he saw the practice of Venerable Yuan. An ancient adept said: “When practice and understanding correspond,
only then can one be called a patriarch. A partial understanding is not sufficient to be a teacher.” As for Venerable Yuan, cultivation and realization were both fully attained,
practice and understanding both perfectly complete; therefore it would be improper not to erect a stele. Moreover, from the former masters Wuqukong, Wuhuan Chong, and Nan
Ming Guang, the three great Venerables, there are also recorded sayings and biographies showing the transmission lineage, and the robe and bowl have already
been made manifest, though not yet engraved in stone; therefore one must carefully arrange the full account and combine it into a stele inscription. The text
reads as follows:

The Venerable Yuan of Yuan Lake, Dharma name Miaoyong, style Xuanwei, his father was Zheng Qiru of Haiyan, Hangzhou,
and his mother was of the Lu family. As a child he naturally ate vegetarian food and delighted in revering the Three Jewels. At twelve he left home and took refuge at Jia
He Monastery Xingchan, taking Jizhou as his master; at seventeen he shaved his head and immediately knew there was a matter beyond the summit. At that time
in the year Wushen, Chan Master Nanming Guang, who had received the Dharma from Chan Master Wuhuan Chong, had originally also
shaved at Xingchan Monastery; just then he was returning with his staff from Jing Mountain to his former temple. The master, upon seeing him, aspired inwardly and said:
“At last I have found my place of refuge.” He then followed him to Yunqi to receive full ordination. Nanming returned to Jing
Mountain, and the master then hurried to Chexi to pay respects to the Old Master Wuhuan and seek instruction, investigating the essential points of Chan. In the following spring,
Nanming and the other masters of Wengu established a meditation retreat at Jing Mountain; the master attended the evening session and heard the raising of the osmanthus
incense case; from then on he exerted himself with such determination that he forgot sleep and food. That autumn, Jing Mountain invited Wuhuan of Chexi to preside over the Dharma,
but within a year Wuhuan showed illness and descended the mountain, appointing Nanming to succeed him; his exhortations became even more intense. One night the master
read the Commentary on the Brahma Heaven Sutra of Vimalakirti and gained insight, immediately presenting a verse on no-birth: “Who can smash iron walls and silver mountains,
daring to break them? The thief-like body suddenly presses forward; we look at each other and realize we were old acquaintances. At that instant, shame and confusion
turn into smiling faces.” He also said: “Through countless kalpas of hardship, the scenery has flowed on until this very day; music and
song, carriages and horses, the gates like a market; yet the inner courtyard remains locked in silence.” Nan read it and sharply reprimanded him,
whereupon he vowed never again to compose verses, and to hold only to his original topic of investigation. In the winter of Xinhai, the master together with Great Master Xueqiao Xin
were sitting around the stove when Xue said: “What is the meaning of a snow lion facing the fire?” The master said: “Dew.” Xue nodded in approval.
In the year Renzi, the master was on the meditation platform, increasingly resolute and deeply mindful of birth and death. After each sitting of incense, when he saw the divided meal
offerings, he would choke and be unable to swallow, and would withdraw behind his seat. In the year Guichou, Nan descended the mountain and closed himself in seclusion at Gaoting Yongqing
Monastery. The master guarded the retreat for four years without the slightest slackness. At that time Venerable Yunmen Zhan opened a Dharma hall at
the Eastern Pagoda of Jiahe; the master went to visit and asked: “There is no Dharma before the eyes, yet the meaning is in what is before the eyes—how is this to be understood?”
Zhan said: “I hear your teacher is about to leave seclusion?” The master said: “If it is not a Dharma before the eyes, and not something within the reach of eyes and ears,
then how is it?” Zhan bowed and said: “Please leave; I wish to enter quiet sitting.” The master then withdrew.丙辰
At Longju a meditation session was established, and Nan was invited out of seclusion to preside. At that time Chan Master Shiyu Fang was studying under Nanming,
and the master traveled with him to Baixia and Hecheng, where they were closely in accord. In the autumn of Wuwu, Nan fell ill; the master served him as attendant, preparing medicine and decoctions
for three years without a trace of weariness. When Nan descended the mountain to seek medical treatment at Jituo Hermitage in Hecheng, one day he raised
Xiangyan’s verse to question the master; as the master was about to answer, Nan shouted, and when he tried again to speak, Nan shouted once more; the master
nodded and shed tears. Nan then entrusted him with a verse: “No transmission, no receiving Dharma; no transmission, no receiving mind;
it is entrusted to the one without hands, who snaps the sinews of empty space.” He further instructed: “You must diligently maintain it; in the future
if you obtain even one or half a person, you will not fail the entrustment of former worthies.” Soon after, Nan passed away. This was the moment when the great master Yuan
received Dharma in the inner chamber through encounter and realization. As for the lineage and teaching sayings handed down from above that can be systematically recorded,
It is from Chan Master Duanqiao Lun that this line of transmission first branches off. Duanqiao and Xueyan Qin both came from Master Wuzhun Fan
Chan Master’s gate; both are descendants of the Linji lineage, all tied to the Buddha and Patriarchs’ life of wisdom, not a matter of side or main, nor of severed
or continuation that could admit partial views. From Wuzhun’s transmission to Duanqiao, the Dharma verse says: “True principle is straight as
a string; what need is there for silence or speech? I now well entrust this, showing that mind is originally without attainment.” Duanqiao transmitted to Fang
Shan Bao, and down to Wuqu Kong, there are ten generations of transmission verses, fully recorded in the Record of the Lamp, with no
Wuqu entrusted Wuhuan Chong, saying: “The master transmits the lineage of the Flower Picking, showing me the Dharma of the subtle smile, and personally hands it down to
you, to uphold and offer it throughout all dust realms.” Wuhuan entrusted Nanming Guang, saying: “To attain the source is to have nothing attained; transmission is also
nothing to transmit. Now I entrust the Dharma of no transmission; east and west share the same sky.” This is the lineage of Dharma transmission received by the great master Yuan from above.
As for the source of the Dharma; regarding the master’s conduct after inheriting the Dharma, he skillfully concealed throughout the entire course of hidden
guidance and secret practice, there is still more that can be recorded. When the master’s teacher Nan passed away, after completing the posthumous affairs, though he had received
heavy entrustment, he never fully accepted it for himself, but dwelt in quiet detachment and indifference, staying in seclusion at Xiawu Mountain for a long time. One day
he read Yongming’s verse: “An illusory person asks the illusory adept; valley echoes answer the sound of springs. If you wish to know the meaning of my school,
the clay ox walks upon water.” Thus he became freely unobstructed. He also read Baiyun Duan’s pointer on awakening at the Fifth Patriarch
Yan’s episode, and for the first time clearly understood the descending clear breeze. At that time Master Xue, according to an old agreement, invited him to reside at Jing Mountain
Thousand-Monk Hermitage; the master went there and stayed for three months. One night during the waxing moon while viewing the moon, Xue pointed to the moon and said
“Where has this half gone?” After a long silence the master said: “Do you understand?” Xue said: “It is still only half
a moon.” The master said: “Where has this half gone?” Xue again fell silent. The master said: “It is still only half
a moon.” They both laughed heartily. In the spring of Jiazi he descended the mountain, returned to Xingchan and closed off retreat for two years. Hearing that Miyun
Wu had opened a Dharma hall, Master Jinsu went to pay respects and said: “I have come specially to have a personal audience.” Miyun raised his fist and said: “Do you see it?”
The master said: “I see.” Miyun said: “What do you see?” The master said: “Everyone is right here.” Miyun then ceased.
He was then invited to serve as head of the assembly; after the term ended he retired to seclusion at Lu Hermitage by Longshui, with sparse fences and broken huts, living alone
for two years. Each day he wore a hemp robe and kasaya, went village by village holding his alms bowl, following the ritual of the Seven Buddhas, teaching with equality
and transforming beings. At meal times he would always offer to birds, animals, and even creatures of the deep, explaining the precepts; at the two daily offerings to spirits and ghosts, his food was especially
reverent and careful. The master was often ill and his body could hardly bear his robes, yet he maintained utmost simplicity; when offerings of fruit were presented, he never privately
partook of them. Even if a few coins’ worth of water or fermented steamed food lasted four days, tea was always reboiled and re-dried, and his feet were wrapped in ragged robes
left over. Master Liang of Gaoting offered a new foot-wrapping cloth. The master said: “Even the sincere respect of a donor is hard to accept,
how much less shared clothing?” Liang blushed with shame and bowed again; from then on he also did not casually make new garments. A
certain master presented incense money in homage, but the master firmly refused. He then moved to Tongyue Hermitage by Yuan River, which is
now Ruru Hermitage of Chan Master Wanru. In his early years Wan also left lay life at Xingchan and was a senior disciple of the master,
sharing the same succession of great good teachers with the master, tracing back to Nanming, and the three masters stayed at Yuan
River for several years; this is the origin of the name Yuanhu. At that time, a group including the Licentiate Xu Zhenhou
planned to form a practice association. The Chan master urged them with plain and direct guidance, did not engage in unnecessary affairs, and declined them. In the Chongzhen
years Gengwu and Xinwei, Layman Liyan made great efforts to restore the ruined Baizhu Puming Temple site.
In the Tianfu era of Later Jin, the site of Chan Master Puming’s monastery had the “Ten Oxherding Verses,” along with
ten illustrations. The Great Master Yunqi printed and transmitted them; this was the very place where that monastery had been established.
Liyan specifically sought those with genuine practice and real awakening, mature and steady, to plant the staff and open the wilderness. Seeing the master
outwardly composed and inwardly detached, and his responses to situations were steady and fitting, he donated funds to build a quiet hut. Local gentry such as Censor Cao
Shicang, Remonstrator Huang Anzhai, and Official Tan Saoan strongly urged the master to reside there and preside. At that time
foxes and hares ran rampant, sparrows and mice quarreled; those who loved him urged the master to withdraw far away, while those who disliked him feared he would flee by night.
The master alone remained untouched by gain and loss, standing firm and unmoved. When the turmoil subsided, he took up his staff and went to Fujian,
in the autumn of Bingzi year. The master concealed his traces at Jiyang Mountain in Jianning for three years; Licentiate Huang
Sumen, Zhu Lianyue and others sought the Way and reached him, and led the assembly to the ruined temple site at Ziyun Mountain
to rebuild the monastery gate and request the master to spread the teaching; the master therefore named the temple Puming and began teaching there.
This was not forgetting the karmic connection with Puming of Zuili, and expressed the meaning of manifesting in all places, just as in the past
he had changed his name to Yuanhu. Not long after spreading the teaching, all neglected matters were restored; the two Chan masters Shiyu and Wanru
were then both abbotting monasteries in Fujian and successively invited the master to ascend the seat; he also visited Shiyu at Lingfeng, where all the donors
gathered requesting him to give a Dharma talk. He raised the whisk and said: “Do you understand? ‘Mind itself is Buddha’ is still confused,
neither mind nor Buddha offers any direction to follow; not mind, not Buddha, not even a thing, like a butterfly weaving through flowers
seen deep within. It cannot be taken, cannot be abandoned; within the unattainable, thus is attainment—like a dragonfly touching water,
gently flying. Puming’s way of expounding thus struck the itch of Lingfeng, recalling the one who had left home
a roaming abode, walking with great ease and breadth; towering and solitary, utterly clean and utterly bare, he trampled down
the crowns of all sages, holding fast the crucial gate. At times he held firm, at times he let go, at times he held firm
while letting go, and at times letting go while still holding firm. Do you know that in a sandalwood forest it is nothing but sandalwood,
and in a lion’s cave there is nothing but lions? Among the assembly, a sharp-witted one stepped forth and said: ‘Puming
has needlessly exposed the hidden essence of Lingfeng.’ The mountain monk only replied to him: ‘It is only because we once shared the same bed
that I thoroughly know where the blanket is torn.’” He struck his staff and descended the seat. From the time the master opened the teaching, those who received his transmission
and continued the lineage were three: Yunfeng Jun, Jiean Jin, and Yichu Yuan. His transmission verse said: “Along the flowing stream
this single matter has neither head nor tail; I entrust it to the lion cubs, whose roaring fills the great earth.” When the master first
resided at Puming in Fujian, there was a prediction that his stay would be limited to three years, and it indeed came to pass. When it reached the Renwu year
in early winter he showed signs of illness and said to the assembly: “In general, those in the Chan community who leave family and cut off attachment and consume alms from the faithful all
rely on the compassionate light of the Three Jewels. The highest are those who investigate Chan and study the Way; next are those who are diligent in both precepts and discipline; and further
are those who resolve their mind to practice; the rest are merely trivial and not worth mentioning. I, this sick and decayed one, have also passed through here
coming and going. Now although wind and fire are about to disperse, I have been able to see through a single matter and not be bound by past conditions, and furthermore
how would this one matter of the sick and decayed be expressed? Clear wind, geese falling, sound after sound of feathers; autumn rain on parasol trees,
mountains with lingering veins. Take care.” He then asked for a brush and bade farewell to the protecting donors, saying: “This poor monk once in earlier years was at the ancestral
court and heard the two words ‘birth and death,’ which are the marrow of Chan. Arriving here, even Chan cannot be obtained,
and birth and death cannot be obtained either. Now as wind and fire disperse, only Puming Chan Monastery remains; it was originally
established by the protecting donors, and now I return it to the protecting donors. I trust the entrustment of Vulture Peak will surely not be forgotten
indeed. Humbly I bid farewell.” He then wrote the verse: “Birth is wrong, death is wrong; the iron lion snaps the golden
chain.” He threw down the brush, gave a shout, and passed away. It was the eleventh day of the tenth month, Chen hour, in the Chongzhen Renwu year,
he having been born on the seventh day of the tenth month, You hour, in the Wanli Dinghai year, with an age of fifty-six years,
and a Dharma seniority of forty-four years. Daoist Sao once said: among those who have received the Dharma in Chan, secret practice is especially important. The Fourth Patriarch
after inheriting the ancestral style, kept his mind gathered without sleep, never lying down on his side for sixty years. The Fifth Patriarch also said:
“The one who receives the robe has a life as if suspended by a thread.” Chan Master Yuansou Duan, when summoned to reside at Mount Jing, for many years the most
long in duration, even up to his passing into quiescence never once loosened his robe to sleep at night; how could it merely be for inheriting the Dharma and teaching as something superior
freely at ease and happy in himself? In the Record of Subtle Illuminations it is recorded that the master’s calligraphy was ancient and austere; he once copied the Avatamsaka Sutra,
and several Mahayana texts of the Prajnaparamita. Whenever paper, brush, or binding materials were needed, he never misused them; scraps
of leftover paper used for drafts were then burned, and not a single character was seen scattered or wasted; in every moment and every situation,
it was nothing other than the mind of the ancient Buddhas. This is utterly different from those who neglect small conduct and thereby ruin great virtue. As for
his power to reverse declining currents, his profound subtlety and hidden silence, and his freedom in coming and going, he is especially a teacher for a hundred generations in the Dharma gate.
is exemplary. Therefore it is fitting to compose this inscription. The inscription says:

Broken Bridge stone, iron within stone; Chexi water, moon within water. Refining stone to mend the sky, yet the sky is never lacking; pointing
to the moon, the stream is imprinted and the stream is never exhausted. It can make a rolled stone stand for Mount Tai’s peaks, and a drop of water deepen Caoxi
gorge. Alas! In Xingchan Monastery, generation after generation there are fallen lions buried without yin or yang grave openings. From Che arises Yuan
Lake, shaking the long source; the bridge connects Puming, shining upon the ancient tracks.

On the winter solstice day of the Jiawu year in the Shunzhi reign
by imperial appointment, Vice Director of the Imperial Academy and Acting Sacrificial Minister, formerly second-class jinshi of the Wuchen year, Ministry of Works
Department of Works, Forestry and Arsenal Bureau Director, and Vice Director of the Left Court of the Supreme Court
written by layman Saoan Tan Zhenmo Pantan, disciple of the Caoxi “Venerable Han” Patriarch, Fuzheng Daoyi
